### getFirstCANdbFilename

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetFirstCANdbFileName.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetFirstCANdbFileName.htm)

**Description:**

Finds out the filename of the first assigned database.


**Syntax:**

```c
dword getFirstCANdbFilename( char buffer[], dword size);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `buffer` | Buffer in which the database filename is written. |
| `size` | Size of the buffer in Byte. |


**Return Value:** If successful unequal 0, otherwise 0.


**Example(s):**

```c
on start { char buffer[256]; dword pos; pos = GetFirstCANdbFilename( buffer, elcount( buffer)); //Finds the filename of the first database. //If a database is found, "pos" contains the value 1. //If none is found "pos" contains 0. while ( 0 != pos) { write( "CANdb: %s", buffer); pos = GetNextCANdbFilename( pos, buffer, elcount( buffer)); //Finds the filenames of other databases. //If any other databases are found //"pos" contains the value 2, 3, etc //If no further databases are found //"pos" contains 0 and the loop is exited } } Example to find the third database on key '3' { char buffer[256]; dword pos; dword DbcNumber = 2; //Position number of the second database pos = GetNextCANdbFilename(DbcNumber, buffer, elcount(buffer)); //Returns the filename of the third database. //Return value "pos" contains the value 3. //If no third database is found "pos" contains 0. write( "Database position number : %d Database file name : %s",pos, buffer); }
```


---

Finds out the name of the first assigned database.


**Syntax:**

```c
dword getFirstCANdbName( char buffer[], dword size)
```

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `buffer` | Buffer in which the database name is written. |
| `size` | Size of the buffer in Byte. |


**Return Value:** If successful unequal 0, otherwise 0.


**Example(s):**

```c
on start { char buffer[256]; dword pos; pos = GetFirstCANdbName( buffer, elcount( buffer)); //Finds the name of the first database. //If a database is found, "pos" contains the value 1 //If none is found "pos" contains 0 while ( 0 != pos) { write( "CANdb: %s", buffer); pos = GetNextCANdbName( pos, buffer, elcount( buffer)); //Finds the names of other databases. //If any other databases are found //"pos" contains the value 2, 3, etc //If no further databases are found //"pos" contains 0 and the loop is exited } } Example to find the third database on key '3' { char buffer[256]; dword pos; dword DbcNumber = 2; //Position number of the second database pos = GetNextCANdbName(DbcNumber, buffer, elcount(buffer)); //Returns the name of the third database. //Return value "pos" contains the value 3. //If no third database is found "pos" contains 0. write( "Database position number : %d Database name : %s",pos, buffer); }
```

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetSignalName.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetSignalName.htm)

**Description:**

Finds out the signal name at a specified start index inside a CAN message (form 1). Finds out the signal name at a specified start index inside a PDU (form 2).


**Syntax:**

```c
char[] getSignalName(caplCanMessage* msg, dword startBit); // form 1
```

```c
char[] getSignalName(caplAutosarPDU* pdu, dword startBit); // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `msg` | CAN message. |
| `pdu` | AUTOSAR PDU. |
| `startBit` | First bit of the signal of the AUTOSAR PDU or CAN message. |


**Return Value:** Returns the signal name at a given start index inside a CAN message. Start index is defined in DBC Editor (form 1). Returns signal name at a given start index inside a PDU. Start index is defined with AUTOSAR Explorer (form 2).


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```

### getMessageAttrInt

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetMessageAttrInt.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetMessageAttrInt.htm)

**Description:**

Gets the value of a message attribute from the database. A user-defined attribute with the name specified in the parameter, and of the Integer type, must be defined in the database. If no such attribute is defined, the function returns 0. If no attribute value is assigned to the message in the database, the default value of the attribute definition is returned.


**Syntax:**

```c
long GetMessageAttrInt(message canMessage, char attributeName[]);
```

```c
long GetMessageAttrInt(pg parameterGroup, char attributeName[]);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `canMessage` | Message variable |
| `attributeName` | Attribute name |


**Return Value:** Value of the attribute (or default value) from the database.


**Example(s):**

```c
on message * { long cycleTimeValue1; long cycleTimeValue2; cycleTimeValue1 = getMessageAttrInt(this, "GenMsgCycleTime"); write("CycleTime of message id %x = %d", this.id, cycleTimeValue1); message EngineData gMsgEngineData; cycleTimeValue2 = getMessageAttrInt(gMsgEngineData, "GenMsgCycleTime"); write("CycleTime of message id %x = %d", this.id, cycleTimeValue2); }
```


---

### getMessageID

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetMessageID.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetMessageID.htm)

**Description:**

Finds out the message ID


**Syntax:**

```c
dword getMessageID(char messageName[]); // form 1
```

```c
dword getMessageID(char messageName[], char dbName[]); // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `messageName` | Name of the message. |
| `dbName` | Name of the database, needed if the message name is used in more than one database. |


**Return Value:** Message ID, or (dword)-1 if the message is not found


**Example(s):**

```c
dword id; id = GetMessageID("LightState");
```


---

### getMessageName

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetMessageName.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetMessageName.htm)

**Description:**

Finds out the message name.


**Syntax:**

```c
dword getMessageName( dword id, dword context, char buffer[], dword size)
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `id` | Id of the message for which the message name should be found. |
| `context` | Context of assigned databases. The low word of context contains the channel number. The high word of context contains the bus system. Available bus systems: CAN 1 LIN 5 FlexRay 7 J1708 9 |
| `CAN` | 1 |
| `LIN` | 5 |
| `FlexRay` | 7 |
| `J1708` | 9 |
| `buffer` | Buffer in which the message name is written. |
| `size` | Size of the buffer in Byte. |


**Return Value:** If successful unequal 0, otherwise 0.


**Example(s):**

```c
variables { dword contextCAN = 0x00010000; dword contextLIN = 0x00050000; dword contextFLEXRAY = 0x00070000; dword contextBEAN = 0x00080000; dword contextJ1708 = 0x00090000; } on message * { char buffer[64]; if ( getMessageName( this.ID, contextCAN | this.CAN, buffer, elcount( buffer))) { write( "Message: %s", buffer); } }
```


---

### getNextCANdbFilename

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetNextCANdbFileName.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetNextCANdbFileName.htm)

**Description:**

Finds out the filenames of the other assigned databases with pos .


**Syntax:**

```c
dword getNextCANdbFilename( dword pos, char buffer[], dword size);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `pos` | Position number of the database to be found. |
| `buffer` | Buffer in which the database filename is written. |
| `size` | Size of the buffer in Byte. |


**Return Value:** If successful unequal 0, otherwise 0.


**Example(s):**

```c
on start { char buffer[256]; dword pos; pos = getFirstCANdbFilename( buffer, elcount( buffer)); //Finds the filename of the first database. //If a database is found, "pos" contains the value 1. //If none is found "pos" contains 0. while ( 0 != pos) { write( "CANdb: %s", buffer); pos = getNextCANdbFilename( pos, buffer, elcount( buffer)); //Finds the filenames of other databases. //If any other databases are found //"pos" contains the value 2, 3, etc //If no further databases are found //"pos" contains 0 and the loop is exited } } Example to find the third database on key '3' { char buffer[256]; dword pos; dword DbcNumber = 2; //Position number of the second database pos = getNextCANdbFilename(DbcNumber, buffer, elcount(buffer)); //Returns the filename of the third database. //Return value "pos" contains the value 3. //If no third database is found "pos" contains 0. write( "Database position number : %d Database file name : %s",pos, buffer); }
```


---

### getNextCANdbName

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetNextCANdbName.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetNextCANdbName.htm)

Finds out the names of the other assigned databases with pos .

```c
dword getNextCANdbName( dword pos, char buffer[], dword size)
```

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `pos` | Position number of the database to be found. |
| `buffer` | Buffer in which the database name is written. |
| `size` | Size of the buffer in Byte. |


**Return Value:** If successful unequal 0, otherwise 0.


**Example(s):**

```c
on start { char buffer[256]; dword pos; pos = getFirstCANdbName( buffer, elcount( buffer)); //Finds the name of the first database. //If a database is found, "pos" contains the value 1. //If none is found "pos" contains 0. while ( 0 != pos) { write( "CANdb: %s", buffer); pos = getNextCANdbName( pos, buffer, elcount( buffer)); //Finds the names of other databases. //If any other databases are found //"pos" contains the value 2, 3, etc //If no further databases are found //"pos" contains 0 and the loop is exited } } Example to find the third database on key '3' { char buffer[256]; dword pos; dword DbcNumber = 2; //Position number of the second database pos = getNextCANdbName(DbcNumber, buffer, elcount(buffer)); //Returns the name of the third database. //Return value "pos" contains the value 3. //If no third database is found "pos" contains 0. write( "Database position number : %d Database name : %s",pos, buffer); }
```

### setSignalStartValues

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionSetSignalStartValues.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionSetSignalStartValues.htm)

**Description:**

Sets the values of the signals in the parameter to the start values defined in the database.


**Syntax:**

```c
setSignalStartValues(message msg); // form 1
```

```c
setSignalStartValues(multiplexed_message msg); // form 2
```

```c
setSignalStartValues(frFrame frame); // form 3
```

```c
setSignalStartValues(frFrame frame, byte uninitializedData); // form 4
```

```c
setSignalStartValues(frPDU pdu); // form 5
```

```c
setSignalStartValues(frPDU pdu, byte uninitializedData); // form 6
```

```c
setSignalStartValues(pg paramGroup); // form 7
```

```c
setSignalStartValues(j1587Param param); // form 8
```

```c
setSignalStartValues(linFrame msg); // form 9
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `msg, frame, pdu, paramGroup, parameter` | Objects where the signals shall be set. |
| `uninitializedData (Form 4, Form 6)` | Value to which the bytes in the frame / PDU shall be set which are not used by signals. |


**Return Value:** 0 function was successful 1 message / frame / PDU / paramGroup wasn't found in the database 2 at least one signal start value didn't fit into the signal in the message


**Example(s):**

```c
message LightState msg; setSignalStartValues(msg);
```

### on busIntegration

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/EventProcedures/CAPLfunctionOnBusIntegration.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/EventProcedures/CAPLfunctionOnBusIntegration.htm)

**Description:**

This event handler is called when a bus integration event occurs on a CAN channel. The event occurs when the CAN channel is activated after the measurement has started or when the channel is reset.


**Syntax:**

```c
on busIntegration( ); // form 1
```

```c
on CAN*.busIntegration( ); // form 2
```


**Example(s):**

*Example form 1*

```c
Form 1 (Wildcard all CAN channels the bus integration event occurs) on busIntegration { write("Channel %d Time %llu", this.channel, this.time_ns); }
```

*Example form 2*

```c
Form 2 (Defined CAN channel the bus integration event occurs) on CAN1.busIntegration { write("Channel %d Time %llu", this.channel, this.time_ns); }
```


---

### on errorFrame

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/EventProcedures/CAPLfunctionOnErrorFrame.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/EventProcedures/CAPLfunctionOnErrorFrame.htm)

**Description:**

The error frame handler is called after receiving an Error Frame or an Overload Frame. If a CAN FD ISO-enabled network interface is used, the handler will also be called on occurrence of a protocol exception. The protocol exception is signaled either if the CAN controller is set to restricted mode and an error occurs, or if the protocol exception handling is enabled. In the former case the error code can be used to find out the type of error. In the latter case the error code is set to protocol exception . This means, that either the CAN controller has received a CAN FD frame in classical CAN mode (the FDF bit of the frame is recessive), or the res-Bit in a CAN FD frame is recessive.


**Example(s):**

```c
on errorFrame { const int bufferSize = 256; char buffer[bufferSize]; char cdirection[2][3] = {"RX", "TX"}; int ndir; word ecc; word extInfo; int isProtocolException; ecc = (this.ErrorCode >> 6) & 0x3f; extInfo = (this.ErrorCode >> 12) & 0x3; isProtocolException = (this.ErrorCode & (1 << 15)) != 0; ndir = extInfo == 0 || extInfo == 2 ? 0 : 1; //set ndir to 0 for RX and to 1 for TX if(this.CtrlType == 1){ //SJA1000 specific switch (ecc){ case 0: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "Bit error"); break; case 1: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "Form error"); break; case 2: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "Stuff error"); break; case 3: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "Other error"); break; default: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "Unknown error code"); } } else if(this.CtrlType == 2){ //CAN core specific switch (ecc){ case 0: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "Bit error"); break; case 1: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "Form error"); break; case 2: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "Stuff error"); break; case 3: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "Other error"); break; case 4: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "CRC error"); break; case 5: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "ACK Del. error"); break; case 7: { switch (extInfo){ case 0: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "RX NACK error (recessive error flag)"); break; case 1: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "TX NACK error (recessive error flag)"); break; case 2: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "RX NACK error (dominant error flag)"); break; case 3: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "TX NACK error (dominant error flag)"); break; } break; } case 8: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "Overload frame"); break; case 9: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "FDF or res recessive"); break;  //protocol exception specific default: snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "Unknown error code"); break; } } else snprintf(buffer, bufferSize, "Unsupported CAN controller"); if(isProtocolException){ write("Protocol exception on CAN%d at %fs: %s", this.can, this.time/1e5, buffer); } else{ write("%s error frame on CAN%d at %fs: %s", cdirection[ndir], this.can, this.time/1e5, buffer); } }
```

### Events of the CAN Controller

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/EventProcedures/CAPLfunctionsEventproceduresCANController.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/EventProcedures/CAPLfunctionsEventproceduresCANController.htm)

### canConfigureBusOff

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanConfigureBusOff.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanConfigureBusOff.htm)

**Description:**

Uses the defined ID of a message to set the bus state to BusOff . Note This function requires minimum a Vector driver 9.6 and a network interface with ISO CAN FD support.


**Syntax:**

```c
long canConfigureBusOff(long channel, long canId, long flags);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `Channel` | The CAN channel. |
| `canId` | Message ID that is used to set the bus state to BusOff . Note If the value -1 is used for this parameter, all IDs are used to reach the bus state Bus Off . |
| `flags` | 0 = Switches off the disturbance 1 = Switches on the disturbance |


**Return Value:** 1 The disturbance for the defined ID was switched on successfully. 0 The disturbance for the defined ID could not switched on.


**Example(s):**

```c
variables { message 0x1 msg;  // define CAN message } on key '1' { canConfigureBusOff(msg.msgChannel, msg.Id, 1); // enable disturbance } on key '2' { output(msg); } on key '3' { canConfigureBusOff(msg.msgChannel, msg.Id, 0); // disable disturbance } on key 'r' { resetCanEx(1); }
```

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanGetDataLength.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanGetDataLength.htm)

Returns the valid payload length of the can message.


**Syntax:**

```c
long canGetDataLength(message msg);
```

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `msg` | CAN message |


**Return Value:** 0 — 64


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```

### canOutputErrorFrame

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctioncanOutputErrorFrame.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctioncanOutputErrorFrame.htm)

**Description:**

Outputs an Error Frame to the CAN bus. The number of dominant bits and the number of trailing recessive bits are given as arguments.


**Syntax:**

```c
long canOutputErrorFrame(errorFrame, long dominant, long recessive);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `errorFrame` | Variable of type errorFrame. |
| `dominant` | Number of dominant bits. |
| `recessive` | Number of recessive bits. |


**Return Value:** 1 OK 0 error, e.g. not supported by driver


**Example(s):**

```c
canOutputErrorFrame(errorFrame, 12, 0); //output Error Frame with 12 dominant bits on CAN1 canOutputErrorFrame(CAN2.errorFrame, 6, 0); //output Error Frame with 6 dominant bits on CAN2
```


---

### getPayloadData

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetPayloadData.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetPayloadData.htm)

**Description:**

Returns the valid payload of a frame that was interrupted during transmission. Depending on error position valid bits are written to the payload parameter. If there is no payload data, nothing is written into the payload parameter.


**Syntax:**

```c
getPayloadData(errorframe errFrame, byte[] payload, dword payloadSize;
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `errFrame` | Error frame which includes the payload. |
| `payload` | Array for writing the transmitted payload of the error frame. |
| `payloadSize` | Size of the payload parameter. |


**Return Value:** 0 No valid payload. >0 Count of valid bits within the payload array.


**Example(s):**

```c
variables { byte payload[64]; dword validBits; } on errorFrame { int i = 0; byte validPayload[64]; validBits = getPayloadData(this, validPayload, elcount(validPayload)); byteCount = validBits / 8; bitCount = validBits % 8; dataLength =  dlcToDataLength[this.DLC]; for(i = 0; i < byteCount; ++i) { payload[i] = this.byte(i); } }
```

### isStdId, isExtId

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionIsStdLd.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionIsStdLd.htm)

**Description:**

Checks parameter for extended identifier (29 bit) or standard identifier (11 Bit).


**Syntax:**

```c
long isStdId(dword id); // form 1
```

```c
long isStdId(message m); // form 2
```

```c
long isExtId(dword id); // form 3
```

```c
long isExtId(message m); // form 4
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `message` | Variable of type message |
| `id` | Id part of a message |


**Return Value:** 1 if check was successful, else 0


**Example(s):**

```c
... if(isExtId(this)) write("extended identifier"); else write("standard identifier"); or std = isStdId(m100.id);
```

### mkExtId

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionMkExtID.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionMkExtID.htm)

**Description:**

Returns an extended id.


**Syntax:**

```c
dword mkExtId(dword id);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `id` | Id part of a message. |


**Return Value:** Extended identifier


**Example(s):**

```c
... msg.id = mkExtId(this.id); ...
```


---

### output (CAN)

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionOutput.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionOutput.htm)

**Description:**

Outputs a message (form 1) or an Error Frame (form 2) from the program block.


**Syntax:**

```c
void output(message msg); // form 1
```

```c
void output(errorFrame); // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `msg` | Variable of type message. |
| `errorFrame` | Variable of type errorFrame. |


**Example(s):**

*output(msg) Example*

```c
variables { message can2.125 msg = { // define CAN message dlc = 1, byte(0) = 1 }; } on key F1 { output (msg); // output Message }
```

*output(errorFrame) Example*

```c
on key F10 { output(errorFrame); // output Error Frame on CAN channel 1 } on CAN2.errorFrame { output (CAN3.errorFrame); // output Error Frame on CAN channel 3 }
```

> Except for the first channel for all other channels the key word errorFrame has to be qualified with the number of the CAN channel.


---

### valOfId

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionValOfId.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionValOfId.htm)

**Description:**

Returns the value of a message identifier independent of its type. Identifier as long value.


**Syntax:**

```c
long valOfId(dword id);
```

```c
long valOfId(message m);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `message` | Variable of the type message. |
| `id` | Id portion of a message. |


**Return Value:** Identifier as long value.


**Example(s):**

```c
on message * { long x; x = valOfId(this); write("Received Identifier: %d",x); output(this); }
```

### canActivateTxSelfAck

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanActivateTxSelfAck.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanActivateTxSelfAck.htm)

**Description:**

Activates/deactivates the transmit self ack feature for the defined channel.


**Syntax:**

```c
int canActivateTxSelfAck (int channel, int activate);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `channel` | CAN channel |
| `activate` | 0 = deactivate 1 = activate |


**Return Value:** 0 device does not support self ack 1 successful -1 In case of error


**Example(s):**

```c
on key a' { int channel int activate; // Activate TX Self-ACK for CAN channel 1 channel = 1; activate = 1; canActivateTXSelfAck(channel, activate); }
```

### canFdGetConfiguration, canFdSetConfiguration

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanFdSetConfiguration.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanFdSetConfiguration.htm)

**Description:**

The CAN controller parameters for arbitration and data phase can be set or read canFdSetConfiguration performs an automatic reset of the CAN controller.


**Syntax:**

```c
long canFdSetConfiguration(long channel, canSettings abrSettings, canSettings dbrSettings);
```

```c
long canFdGetConfiguration(long channel, canSettings abrSettings, canSettings dbrSettings);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `Channel` | The CAN channel. |
| `struct canSettings` | { float baudrate; 		//in bit/s unsigned char tseg1, tseg2;	//length of the time segments 1 and 2 in time quanta unsigned char sjw;		//sync jump width in time quanta unsigned char sam;		//number of sampling points (1 or 3). Only valid for CAN. For CAN FD "sam" is hard coded to 1. unsigned int flags;		//see description below }; Flags for canFdGetConfiguration : Bit Position Value, Description 0, 1 0: unknown transceiver 1: Low speed 2: Single wire 3: High speed 8 0x100: The channel is configured for CAN FD All others Reserved Flags Bit canFdSetConfiguration : Bit Position Value, Description 0 0: normal mode 1: silent mode (acknowledge not created) All others Reserved, and must be 0. |
| `Bit Position` | Value, Description |
| `0, 1` | 0: unknown transceiver 1: Low speed 2: Single wire 3: High speed |
| `8` | 0x100: The channel is configured for CAN FD |
| `All others` | Reserved |
| `0` | 0: normal mode 1: silent mode (acknowledge not created) |


**Return Value:** 1 success 0 error


**Example(s):**

```c
int ret; int channel = 1; CANsettings abrSettings; CANsettings dbrSettings; abrSettings.baudrate = 1000000; abrSettings.tseg1=5; abrSettings.tseg2=2; abrSettings.sjw=2; abrSettings.sam=1; abrSettings.flags = 0; dbrSettings.baudrate = 4000000; dbrSettings.tseg1=6; dbrSettings.tseg2=3; dbrSettings.sjw=2; dbrSettings.sam=1; dbrSettings.flags = 0; write("Set 1 MB"); ret = canFdSetConfiguration(channel, abrSettings, dbrSettings); if (ret) { write("Arbitration settings: baud= %f, tseg1 = %d, tseg2= %d, sjw = %d, sam = %d, flags = 0x%x",abrSettings.baudrate, abrSettings.tseg1, abrSettings.tseg2, abrSettings.sjw, abrSettings.sam, abrSettings.flags); write("Data settings: baud= %f, tseg1 = %d, tseg2= %d, sjw = %d, sam = %d, flags = 0x%x",dbrSettings.baudrate, dbrSettings.tseg1, dbrSettings.tseg2, dbrSettings.sjw, dbrSettings.sam, dbrSettings.flags); }
```

### canFlushTxQueue

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanFlushTxQueue.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanFlushTxQueue.htm)

**Description:**

Flushes the Tx queue for the defined channel.


**Syntax:**

```c
long canFlushTxQueue(long channel);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `Channel` | The CAN channel. |


**Return Value:** 0 Interface doesn't support flush of Tx queue 1 success -1 error


**Example(s):**

```c
on key 'f' { int result; //flush Tx queue for CAN channel 1 result = canFlushTxQueue(1); if(result == 1) write("Tx queue flushed "); else write("Tx queue flush failed Result =%d ", result); }
```

### canGetConfiguration, canSetConfiguration

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanGetSetConfiguration.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanGetSetConfiguration.htm)

**Description:**

The CAN controller parameters can be set or read. canSetConfiguration performs an automatic reset of the CAN controller.


**Syntax:**

```c
long canGetConfiguration(long channel, canSettings settings);
```

```c
long canSetConfiguration(long channel, canSettings settings);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `Channel` | The CAN channel. |
| `struct canSettings` | { float baudrate; //in bit/s unsigned char tseg1, tseg2; //length of the time segments 1 and 2 in time quanta unsigned char sjw;		//sync jump width in time quanta unsigned char sam; //number of sampling points (1 or 3) unsigned int flags; //see description below }; Flags for canGetConfiguration : Bit Position Value, Description 0, 1 0: unknown transceiver 1: Low speed 2: Single wire 3: High speed 8 0x100: The channel is configured for CAN FD All others Reserved Flags for canSetConfiguration : Bit Position Value, Description 0 0: Normal mode 1: Silent mode (acknowledge not created) All others Reserved, and must be 0. |
| `Bit Position` | Value, Description |
| `0, 1` | 0: unknown transceiver 1: Low speed 2: Single wire 3: High speed |
| `8` | 0x100: The channel is configured for CAN FD |
| `All others` | Reserved |
| `0` | 0: Normal mode 1: Silent mode (acknowledge not created) |


**Return Value:** 1 success 0 error


**Example(s):**

```c
int ret; int channel = 1; canSettings settings; settings.baudrate = 1000000; settings.tseg1=5; settings.tseg2=2; settings.sjw=2; settings.sam=1; settings.flags = 0; write("Set 1 MB"); ret = canSetConfiguration(channel, settings); ret = canGetConfiguration(channel, settings); if (ret) { write("Settings: baud= %f, tseg1 = %d, tseg2= %d, sjw = %d, sam = %d, flags = 0x%x", settings.baudrate, settings.tseg1, settings.tseg2, settings.sjw, settings.sam, settings.flags); }
```


---

### canSetChannelAcc

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanSetChannelAcc.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanSetChannelAcc.htm)

**Description:**

Via an acceptance filter, the CAN controllers control which received messages are sent through to your CANoe DE product . Some controller chips, such as the SJA 1000, expect partitioning into acceptance mask and acceptance code.


**Syntax:**

```c
long canSetChannelAcc(long channel, dword code, dword mask);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `channel` | CAN channel |
| `code` | Acceptance code |
| `mask` | Acceptance mask |


**Return Value:** 0 ok !=0 error


**Example(s):**

```c
on key 'a' { /* To distinguish whether the filter is for standard or extended identifier . For extended identifiers the MSB of the code and mask are set. Description: Different ports may have different filters for a channel. If the CAN hardware cannot implement the filter, the driver virtualises filtering. Accept if ((id ^ code) & mask) == 0). */ long channel =2; dword code=0x10; dword mask=0x10; canSetChannelAcc(channel,code,mask); write("channel mask set"); }
```


---

### canSetChannelMode

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanSetChannelMode.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanSetChannelMode.htm)

**Description:**

Activates/deactivates the TXRQ and Tx of the CAN controller. This function does nothing with the Ack bit.


**Syntax:**

```c
long canSetChannelMode(long channel, long gtx, long gtxreq);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `channel` | CAN channel |
| `gtx` | 0 tx off 1 tx on |
| `0` | tx off |
| `1` | tx on |
| `gtxreq` | 0 gtxreq off 1 gtxreq on |


**Return Value:** 0 ok !=0 error


**Example(s):**

```c
on key 't' { long channel =2; char gtx =1; char gtxreq =1; canSetChannelMode(channel,gtx,gtxreq); Write("Mode set to tx=%d, txreq=%d",gtx,gtxreq); }
```


---

### canSetChannelOutput

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanSetChannelOutput.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionCanSetChannelOutput.htm)

**Description:**

Defines the response of the CAN controller to the bus traffic and sets the ACK bit. The CAN transmitter of the channel is switched off. So no Ack bit is generated, and messages can no longer be sent. It is still possible to receive messages.


**Syntax:**

```c
long canSetChannelOutput(long channel, long silent);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `channel` | CAN channel |
| `silent` | 0 silent 1 normal |
| `0` | silent |
| `1` | normal |


**Return Value:** 0 ok !=0 error


**Example(s):**

```c
on key 's' { long channel =2; long silent =0; canSetChannelOutput(channel,silent); Write("silent set to %d",silent); }
```


---

### GetCanBittimingConfiguration, SetCanBittimingConfiguration

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetSetCanBittimingConfiguration.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetSetCanBittimingConfiguration.htm)

**Description:**

The CAN, CAN FD or CAN-XL controller parameters can be set or read. A automatic reset of the controller is performed.


**Syntax:**

```c
int GetCanBittimingConfiguration(dword channel, BitTiming timing);
```

```c
int GetCanBittimingConfiguration(dword channel, BitTiming[] timings, dword countOfBittimings);
```

```c
int SetCanBittimingConfiguration(dword channel, BitTiming timing);
```

```c
int SetCanBittimingConfiguration(dword channel, BitTiming[] timings, dword countOfBittimings);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `Channel` | The CAN channel. |
| `BitTiming` | The bit timing for the defined phase. |
| `BitTimings` | The bit timings for more than one defined phase. |
| `countofBittimings` | Number of bit timings contained in the array. |


**Return Value:** 0 Successful -1 Invalid bit timing phase -2 Invalid parameter -3 No bit timings available for defined phase -4 No bit timings available for defined channel -255 Unknown error


**Example(s):**

*Example 1*

```c
dword     result; BitTiming timingsToSet; //variable definition BitTiming timingsAreGet; //variable definition //------------------------------------- //Define values for the nominal phase //------------------------------------- timingsToSet.Bitrate = 500000.0; timingsToSet.Tseg1 = 55; timingsToSet.Tseg2 = 24; timingsToSet.Sjw = 24; timingsToSet.BitTimingPhase = eCANNominalPhase; timingsAreGet.BitTimingPhase = eCANNominalPhase; //------------------------------------- //------------------------------------- //Configure the new values //------------------------------------- result = SetCanBittimingConfiguration(1, timingsToSet); if(result == 0) { result = GetCanBittimingConfiguration(1, timingsAreGet); if(result == 0) { write("Settings: Phase = %d bitrate = %f, tseg1 = %d, tseg2= %d, sjw = %d", timingsAreGet.BitTimingPhase, timingsAreGet.Bitrate, timingsAreGet.tseg1, timingsAreGet.tseg2, timingsAreGet.sjw); }
}
```

*Example 2*

```c
dword     result; BitTiming timingsToSet[3]; //variable definition BitTiming timingsAreGet[3]; //variable definition //------------------------------------- //Define values for the nominal phase //------------------------------------- timingsToSet[0].Bitrate = 500000.0; timingsToSet[0].Tseg1 = 55; timingsToSet[0].Tseg2 = 24; timingsToSet[0].Sjw = 24; timingsToSet[0].BitTimingPhase = eCANNominalPhase; timingsAreGet[0].BitTimingPhase = eCANNominalPhase; timingsToSet[1].Bitrate = 1000000.0; timingsToSet[1].Tseg1 = 27; timingsToSet[1].Tseg2 = 12; timingsToSet[1].Sjw = 12; timingsToSet[1].BitTimingPhase = eCANFDPhase; timingsAreGet[1].BitTimingPhase = eCANFDPhase; timingsToSet[2].Bitrate = 4000000.0; timingsToSet[2].Tseg1 = 6; timingsToSet[2].Tseg2 = 3; timingsToSet[2].Sjw = 2; timingsToSet[2].BitTimingPhase = eCANXLPhase; timingsAreGet[2].BitTimingPhase = eCANXLPhase; //------------------------------------- //------------------------------------- //Configure the new values //------------------------------------- result = SetCanBittimingConfiguration(1, timingsToSet, 3); if(result == 0) { result = GetCanBittimingConfiguration(1, timingsAreGet, 3); if(result == 0) { int index; for(index = 0; index < 3; ++index) { write("Settings: Phase = %d bitrate = %f, tseg1 = %d, tseg2=%d, sjw = %d", timingsAreGet[index].BitTimingPhase, timingsAreGet[index].Bitrate, timingsAreGet[index].tseg1, timingsAreGet[index].tseg2, timingsAreGet[index].sjw); } } }
```

### getCardTypeEx

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetCardTypeEx.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetCardTypeEx.htm)

Determines the card type of CAN channel. Is needed e.g. to program the BTR / OCR values.

**Example(s):**

```c
—
```

### getChipType

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetChipType.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionGetChipType.htm)

**Description:**

Determines the type of CAN controller used.


**Syntax:**

```c
long getChipType(long channel);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `CAN channel` | 0 both controller 1 Channel 1 2 Channel 2 |
| `0` | both controller |
| `1` | Channel 1 |
| `2` | Channel 2 |


**Return Value:** Type of controller with the following values: 5 NEC 72005 200 Philipps PCA82C200 526 Intel 82526 527 Intel 82527 1000,1001 Philipps SJA1000 Other types may occur. DEMO versions return the result 0 or simulate one of the existing types. If an attempt is made to access a nonexistent channel (e.g. Channel 2 for CPC/PP) or if the driver used does not support this function, the functional result is 0.


**Example(s):**

```c
... switch(getChipType(0)) { case 200: setOcr(0,0x02); break; case ... default: write("Unknown CAN-chip %d", getChipType(0)); break; } ...
```

### resetCan

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionResetCan.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionResetCan.htm)

**Description:**

Resets the CAN controller. Can be used to reset the CAN controller after a BUSOFF or to activate configuration changes. Since execution of the function takes some time and the CAN controller is disconnected from the bus briefly, messages can be lost when this is performed. Note With this function you can reset CAN1 and CAN2. If only one specific CAN channel is used, resetCan stops with an error and the CAN channels keep offline. In this case the ResetCanEx function has to be used. The function resetCanEx can be used for all channels.


**Syntax:**

```c
void resetCan();
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `—` |  |


**Example(s):**

```c
on key 'r' { // Controller is reset after BUSOFF resetCan(); }
```


---

### ResetCanEx

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionResetCanEx.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionResetCanEx.htm)

**Description:**

Resets the CAN controller for one specific CAN channel. Can be used to reset the CAN controller after a BUSOFF or to activate configuration changes. Since execution of the function takes a certain amount of time and the CAN controller is disconnected from the bus for a brief period messages may be lost. Note The Rx queue of the CAN channel will not be flushed by default.


**Syntax:**

```c
void ResetCanEx(long channel);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `channel` | CAN channel |


**Example(s):**

```c
on key 'r' { // After BUSOFF the controller on Channel 2 is reset resetCanEx(2); }
```


---

### ScanBaudrateActive

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionScanBaudrateActive.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionScanBaudrateActive.htm)

**Description:**

The function determines the baudrate for the given channel. Result of the function is written into the Write Window. The baudrate scanner checks different baudrates and tries to send a message through the given channel. The function is finished if the message was sent successfully and the baudrate was determined. If a wrong baudrate is present, the other power supply takers cannot receive the message. CANoe as the transmitter does not receive an acknowledge and sends an Error Frame. In this case the next baudrate of the baudrate range is checked. Note CAN.INI file settings for the baudrate scanner: [BaudrateScanner] Dlc=8 DisplayBaudrateList=0 Dlc: DLC of the message sent by the scanner. Default value: 8 DisplayBaudrateList: If this value is set to 0 the baudrate scanner stops after finding the first baudrate. If the value is non-zero the scanner checks all baudrates and displays a list of values at the end. Default value: 0


**Syntax:**

```c
long ScanBaudrateActive( dword channel, dword messageID, double firstBaudrate, double lastBaudrate, dword timeout);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `channel` | Channel number. (1,..,32) |
| `messageID` | ID of the message that the scanner will send to detect the baudrate. The DLC of the message is always 8. |
| `firstBaudrate / lastBaudrate` | Baudrate range to scan. If both values are set to zero the scanner checks the most commonly used baudrates: 33.333, 50.0, 83.333, 100.0, 125.0, 250.0, 500.0, 1000.0 [kBaud] If both values are the same but not zero the scanner multiplies the baudrate with a given factor (Value range 0.25-5.0). The factor is changed with steps of 0.25. If both values are different, all possible baudrate values in the ranged are scanned. The incremental step in the range is 1.5%. |
| `timeout` | Period of time [ms] the scanner waits when the message is sent. |


**Return Value:** Returns 0 if the scan function was successfully started. Otherwise the return value is non-zero.


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```


---

### ScanBaudratePassive

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionScanBaudratePassive.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionScanBaudratePassive.htm)

**Description:**

Baudrate scanner checks different baudrates and tries to receive a message on the channel. Function starts the scan and detects the baudrate on the given channel. Result of the function is written into the Write Window. If a wrong baudrate is present, CANoe cannot receive messages and sends an Error Frame, which can be put on the bus using the parameter bAcknowledge . Note CAN.INI file settings for the baudrate scanner: [BaudrateScanner] Dlc=8 DisplayBaudrateList=0 Dlc: DLC of the message sent by the scanner. Default value: 8 DisplayBaudrateList: If this value is set to 0 the baudrate scanner stops after finding the first baudrate. If the value is non-zero the scanner checks all baudrates and displays a list of values at the end. Default value: 0


**Syntax:**

```c
long ScanBaudratePassive( dword channel, dword messageID, double firstBaudrate, double lastBaudrate, dword timeout, dword bAcknowledge);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `channel` | Channel number. (1,..,32) |
| `messageID` | ID of the message that the scanner will receive to detect the baudrate. If this value is 0xffff the scanner will receive all the messages on the channel. |
| `firstBaudrate / lastBaudrate` | Baudrate range to scan. If both values are set to zero the scanner checks the most commonly used baudrates: 33.333, 50.0, 83.333, 100.0, 125.0, 250.0, 500.0, 1000.0 [kBaud] If both values are the same but not zero the scanner multiplies the baudrate with a given factor (Value range 0.25-5.0). The factor is changed with steps of 0.25. If both values are different, all possible baudrate values in the ranged are scanned. The incremental step in the range is 1.5%. |
| `timeout` | Period of time [ms] the scanner waits when the message is sent. |
| `bAcknowledge` | Acknowledge mode on (1)/off (0). If a wrong baudrate is present, CANoe cannot receive messages and sends an Error Frame, which can be put on the bus using the parameter bAcknowledge . The parameter serves for the fact that CANoe - as a passive receiver - can participate indirectly in the network communication by sending an Error Frame. The parameter does not change the acknowledge settings of the Hardware Configuration dialog. The parameter has an effect only during runtime of the function. |


**Return Value:** Returns 0 if the scan function was successfully started. Otherwise the return value is non-zero.


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```


---

### setBtr

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionSetBtr.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionSetBtr.htm)

**Description:**

Sets another baudrate. The values do not become active until the next call of the function resetCan . It should be noted that these values depend on the CAN controller used.


**Syntax:**

```c
long setBtr(long channel, byte btr0, byte btr1);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `CAN channel` | 0 All controllers 1 - 32 channel 1 - 32 |
| `0` | All controllers |
| `1 - 32` | channel 1 - 32 |
| `BTR0` | Value of Bit Timing Register 0. |
| `BTR1` | Value of Bit Timing Register 1. |


**Return Value:** Always 1


**Example(s):**

```c
... setBtr(0, 0x00, 0x3a); // 500 kBaud for 82C200 resetCan(); // activate ...
```


---

### setOcr

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionSetOcr.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CAN/Functions/CAPLfunctionSetOcr.htm)

**Description:**

Sets the Output Control Register. The values do not become active until the next call of the function resetCan() . It should be noted that these values depend on the CAN platform used.


**Syntax:**

```c
setOcr(long channel, byte ocr);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `CAN channel` | 0 all channels > 0 only the given channel |
| `0` | all channels |
| `> 0` | only the given channel |
| `OCR` | Value of the Output Control Register |


**Return Value:** 1 success 0 error


**Example(s):**

```c
... setOcr(0, 0x02); // set resetCan(); // activate ...
```

Returns the current chip state of the CAN x controller. Valid x values: 1…32


| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `—` |  |

See the following table for a description of the return values. 0 Value not available 1 Simulated 2 Not used 3 Error Active 4 Warning Level 5 Error Passive 6 Bus Off A description of the chip states can also be found here: Bus Statistics Window of option CAN.

```c

```

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CANopen/CAPLfunctionsCANopenOverview.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CANopen/CAPLfunctionsCANopenOverview.htm)

### CANopenLssFastScan

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CANopen/CANopenBasic/Functions/CAPLfunctionsCANopenLssFastScan.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/CANopen/CANopenBasic/Functions/CAPLfunctionsCANopenLssFastScan.htm)

**Description:**

The LSS master starts a LSS fast scan to identify an unconfigured LSS slave. The callback is called if an unconfigured slave is found during the scan.


**Syntax:**

```c
dword CANopenLssFastScan (dword vendorID, dword productCode, dword revisionNumber, dword timeout);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `channelID` | Number of the CAN channel. |
| `vendorID` | Vendor-ID of the device. |
| `productCode` | Product code of the device. |
| `revisionNumber` | Revision number of the device. |
| `serialNumber` | Serial number of the device. |
| `timeout` | Distance between each LSS message of the fast scan in ms. |


**Return Value:** 0 Successful 1 Invalid channel (not CAN or inactive)


**Example(s):**

```c
on key 's' { //Start a LSS fast scan from vendor-ID 5, product code 0 and revision number 1 CANopenLssFastScan(5, 0, 1, 10); } void OnCANopenLssFastScan(dword channelID, dword vendorID, dword productCode, dword revisionNumber, dword serialNumber) { Write("LSS slave (%I32u, %I32u, %I32u, %I32u) found on channel %I32u", vendorID, productCode, revisionNumber, serialNumber, channelID); }
```

### on ptpSyncPoint

### ptpGetPtpInstance

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Functions/CAPLfunctionPtpGetPtpInstance.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Functions/CAPLfunctionPtpGetPtpInstance.htm)

**Description:**

Returns the PTP instance associated with the given port and time domain. If the port is a measurement port, the corresponding physical hardware port must be configured as providing or receiving port of a PTP clock in the Vector Hardware Manager . If the port is a virtual port, the virtual port must be assigned to the same segment as a PTP port configured in the clock. In the following networks and clocks layout, the time aware bridge using physical ports 9 and 11 may be accessed using all of Ethernet1::Port9, Ethernet1::Port11, Ethernet1::BridgeNode1, Ethernet1::BridgeNode2 or Ethernet1::EthernetIG. Calling ptpGetPtpInstance with each of these ports will always return the same object. Accessing PTP instances will fail if another application is already accessing the same PTP instance with read-write permissions. PTP instances can only be accessed in network-based mode . Note that only a single time domain is currently supported.


**Syntax:**

```c
PtpInstance ptpGetPtpInstance(ethernetPort port, byte domainNumber);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `port` | A measurement or virtual port. |
| `domainNumber` | The time domain number. |


**Return Value:** PtpInstance The PTP instance. Check with it's property isValid if the call succeeded.


**Example(s):**

```c
PtpInstance ptp1; ptp1 = ptpGetPtpInstance(ethernetport::Ethernet1::Port1, 0); if (ptp1.isValid) { // use methods of PtpInstance to manipulate or query the PTP clock }
```


---

### PtpInstance::AttachTimer

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Methods/CAPLfunctionPtpInstanceAttachTimer.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Methods/CAPLfunctionPtpInstanceAttachTimer.htm)

**Description:**

Assigns the specified CAPL timer or mstimer to a PTP instance. Attaching a timer to a PTP instance means that the timer will now be running in the time scale of the PTP instance's free-running, internal clock. After the timer has been attached, it can be set using the regular CAPL functions setTimer , setTimerCyclic and setAbsTimer . Setting the timer to a timeout period will make it fire after the period has passed in the PTP clock, and is subject to the rateRatio of the PTP clock. Setting the timer to a absolute value means that it will be executed after the PTP clock reaches the specified due time. Note Note that an attached timer no longer has a direct relation to the progress of or steps in the simulation time. E.g. it can not be assumed that when the timer fires, the simulation time will have a determinate value. It is important to note that Speaking more precisely, the PTP time will already have advanced slightly in relation to the simulation time, when the timer gets set. When the timer’s due time is checked in the simulation loop, the current PTP time has, again, already advanced slightly in relation to the current simulation time. Given this behavior, an attached PTP timer may be more precise in terms of (real) PTP time than regular simulation timers. The difference in simulation times between when the timer has been set and when it executes will not be precisely the period specified. The methods SimulationTimeFromSynchronizedTime or SynchronizedTimeFromSimulationTime may be used to translate between the current PTP time and simulation time, or vice versa. Once a timer is attached to a PTP instance, it cannot be detached. An already set timer cannot be attached.


**Syntax:**

```c
byte PtpInstance.AttachTimer(timer t);
```

```c
byte PtpInstance.AttachTimer(mstimer t);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `timer t` | The timer to attach to the PTP instance. |
| `mstimer t` | The millisecond timer to attach to the PTP instance. |


**Return Value:** byte 1 if the call succeeded, 0 otherwise.


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```


---

### PtpInstance::SimulationTimeFromSynchronizedTime

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Methods/CAPLfunctionPtpInstanceSimulationTimeFromSynchronizedTime.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Methods/CAPLfunctionPtpInstanceSimulationTimeFromSynchronizedTime.htm)

**Description:**

Calculates the simulation time from a given PTP time in the timescale of the PTP instance. The ptp time can only be correctly calculated given the following conditions: Because the calculation depends on the data (PTP offset and rate ratio) specified in the sync point event, the simulation time cannot be calculated from a PTP time if the conditions above are not met. In this case, 0 will be returned.


**Syntax:**

```c
int64 PtpInstance .SimulationTimeFromSynchronizedTime(int64 ptpTime);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `simulationTime` | The PTP time to translate. |


**Return Value:** int64 The simulation time corresponding to the given PTP time.


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```


---

### PtpInstance::SimulationTimespanFromSynchronizedTimespan

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Methods/CAPLfunctionPtpInstanceSimulationTimespanFromSynchronizedTimespan.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Methods/CAPLfunctionPtpInstanceSimulationTimespanFromSynchronizedTimespan.htm)

**Description:**

Calculates the simulation time span from a given PTP time span in the timescale of the PTP instance. The PTP time span can only be correctly calculated after at least one ptpSyncPoint event has been received. The calculation will be done using the rate ratio of the last sync point event.


**Syntax:**

```c
int64 PtpInstance .SimulationTimespanFromSynchronizedTimespan (int64 ptpTimeSpan);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `ptpTimeSpan` | The PTP time span to translate. |


**Return Value:** int64 The simulation time span corresponding to the given PTP time span.


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```

### PtpInstance::SynchronizedTimeFromSimulationTime

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Methods/CAPLfunctionPtpInstanceSynchronizedTimeFromSimulationTime.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Methods/CAPLfunctionPtpInstanceSynchronizedTimeFromSimulationTime.htm)

**Description:**

Calculates the PTP time in the timescale of the PTP instance from a given simulation time. The simulation time can only be correctly calculated given the following conditions: Because the calculation depends on the data (PTP offset and rate ratio) specified in the sync point event, the PTP time cannot be calculated from a simulation time if the conditions above are not met. In this case, 0 will be returned.


**Syntax:**

```c
int64 PtpInstance .SynchronizedTimeFromSimulationTime(int64 simulationTime);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `simulationTime` | The simulation time to translate. |


**Return Value:** int64 The PTP time corresponding to the given simulation time.


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```

### PtpInstance::SynchronizedTimespanFromSimulationTimespan

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Methods/CAPLfunctionPtpInstanceSynchronizedTimespanFromSimulationTimespan.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Methods/CAPLfunctionPtpInstanceSynchronizedTimespanFromSimulationTimespan.htm)

**Description:**

Calculates the PTP time span in the timescale of the PTP instance from a given simulation time span. The simulation time span can only be correctly calculated after at least one ptpSyncPoint event has been received. The calculation will be done using the rate ratio of the last sync point event.


**Syntax:**

```c
int64 PtpInstance .SynchronizedTimespanFromSimulationTimespan (int64 simulationTimeSpan);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `simulationTimeSpan` | The simulation time span. |


**Return Value:** int64 The PTP time span corresponding to the given simulation time span.


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```

### PtpInstance

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Objects/CAPLfunctionPtpInstance.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Objects/CAPLfunctionPtpInstance.htm)

**Description:**

Defines a variable of type PtpInstance . A correctly initialized PtpInstance provides direct access to a AVB or AUTOSAR clock configured on a compatible VN hardware. A PtpInstance can be used to query or modify clock properties and provides access to the PTP ports assigned to the PTP instance. A PtpInstance must be initialized using the function ptpGetPtpInstance . Validity can be verified using the selector isValid . A PTP instance can be a PTP leader, follower or a time-aware bridge. The role of a PTP instance is merely defined by its port configuration. A PTP instance having only a receiving port is a PTP follower. If it has only providing ports, it's a leader. Having both, it’s a time-aware bridge (or PTP relay). Setting PtpInstance properties is generally only possible for PTP leader instances, not for follower nor for PTP relay instances. In this case, a warning message will be written to the CANoe DE product Write Window. Some PtpInstance properties are inter dependent on each other. For instance, leap59, leap61 and utcOffset may get recalculated by the hardware if utcOffsetValid is enabled. The timescale cannot currently be changed.


**Syntax:**

```c
PtpInstance ptp;
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `—` |  |


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```


---

### PtpPort

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Objects/CAPLfunctionPtpPort.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/IP/Objects/CAPLfunctionPtpPort.htm)

**Description:**

Define a variable of type PtpPort . A correctly initialized PtpPort provides access to a providing or receiving port of a configured AVB or AUTOSAR clock on a compatible VN hardware. A PtpPort can be used to query or modify clock port properties. A PtpPort must be initialized using the function PtpInstance.GetPtpPort . Validity can be verified using the selector isValid . A PtpPort defines the properties of PTP messaging on a single physical port, such as message intervals, residence and link delays.


**Syntax:**

```c
PtpPort ptpPort;
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `—` |  |


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```
