## RS232 {#rs232}


---

### RS232Close

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232Close.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232Close.htm)

**Description:**

Closes a serial port. The serial port will be closed for all nodes. One and the same port may be closed by several nodes (or repetitively). After closure other programs may use the serial port. After closing the serial port the configuration of the port is lost. The next time the port is opened it will be configured with the system default.


**Syntax:**

```c
dword RS232Close( dword port );
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `port` | A number between 1 and 255 identifying a serial port. |


**Return Value:** 0 error The error occurs if the serial port with the given number does not exist on the system. 1 success


**Example(s):**

```c
if ( 1==RS232Close(1) ) write("It works with port 1.");
```


---

### RS232Open

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232Open.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232Open.htm)

**Description:**

Opens a serial port. Note The serial port is available on exactly the node which executed the CAPL code. One and the same port may be opened by several nodes. If the port has been opened, other programs cannot access the serial port any more.


**Syntax:**

```c
dword RS232Open( dword port );
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `port` | A number between 1 and 255 identifying a serial port. |


**Return Value:** 0 error The error occurs if 1 success


**Example(s):**

```c
if ( 1==RS232Open(1) ) write("It works with port 1.");
```


---

### RS232Configure

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232Configure.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232Configure.htm)

**Description:**

Configures a serial port. Without setting up a configuration explicitly, the default configuration is used. Default baudrate: 9600, 8 data bits, 1 stop bit, no parity. If a deprecated INI file exists, the data of the INI file will be used. With form 2 of this function you can configure a serial port to use parity without letting the operating system verify parity correctness. This form should only be used if the communication to a device was not possible when using parity and form 1 of this function.


**Syntax:**

```c
dword RS232Configure( dword port,  dword baudrate, dword numberOfDataBits, dword numberOfStopBits, dword parity ); // form 1
```

```c
dword RS232Configure( dword port, dword baudrate, dword numberOfDataBits, dword numberOfStopBits, dword parity, dword enableParityCheck); // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `port` | A number between 1 and 255 identifying a serial port. |
| `baudrate` | The symbol rate to use for reception and transmission. Typically, 9600 is used. There is a list of possible values which depends on the serial port. Normally, 115.200 is the allowed maximum. |
| `numberOfDataBits` | The number of data bits within a transmission frame. 8 is used at most. Only values between 5 and 8 are possible. Not all values and not all combinations with other frame parameters may be supported by the serial port. |
| `numberOfStopBits` | A code which sets the number of stop bits within a transmission frame. 1 1 stop bit is used ( 1 has changed meaning compared to deprecated variant RS232SetCommState ) 2 2 stop bits are used Note 1.5 stop bits are not supported any more. |
| `1` | 1 stop bit is used ( 1 has changed meaning compared to deprecated variant RS232SetCommState ) |
| `2` | 2 stop bits are used |
| `parity` | A code which identifies the parity mode to use. 0 no parity used, i.e. frame contains no parity bit 1 odd parity 2 even parity |
| `0` | no parity used, i.e. frame contains no parity bit |
| `enableParityCheck` | Indicates whether to enable or disable the parity verification. This parameter is ignored if the parameter parity is set to 0 (no parity used). 0 parity verification disabled !=0 (≠0) parity verification enabled |
| `!=0 (≠0)` | parity verification enabled |


**Return Value:** 0 error The error occurs if 1 success


**Example(s):**

```c
if ( 0!=RS232Configure(1,9600,8,1,0) ) write("Set typical default at port 1.");
```


---

### RS232SetHandshake

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232SetHandshake.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232SetHandshake.htm)

**Description:**

Sets handshake parameters on serial port. Note If you use hardware handshake, then the functions which change signal lines directly ( RS232SetSignalLine , RS232EscapeCommFunc, RS232EscapeCommExt) may conflict with the automatic handshaking.


**Syntax:**

```c
dword RS232SetHandshake ( dword port, dword handshake, dword XonLimit, dword XoffLimit, dword XonChar, dword XoffChar );
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `port` | A number between 1 and 255 identifying a serial port. |
| `handshake` | Sets variant of handshake to use. Allowed values: 0 no handshake at all 1 hardware handshake, use DTR<->DSR 2 hardware handshake, use RTS<->CTS 3 hardware handshake, use RTS<->CTS, use "toggle" variant 4 software handshake, use Xon and Xoff characters DTR: Data-Terminal-Ready (from sender). RTS: Request-To-Send (from sender). DSR: Data-Set-Ready (from receiver). CTS: Clear-To-Send (from receiver). Note Mixtures of hardware and software handshake are not supported. For sake of compatibility, it is possible to set signal lines to fixed levels with this function. Please prefer for that purpose RS232SetSignalLine . |
| `0` | no handshake at all |
| `1` | hardware handshake, use DTR<->DSR |
| `2` | hardware handshake, use RTS<->CTS |
| `3` | hardware handshake, use RTS<->CTS, use "toggle" variant |
| `4` | software handshake, use Xon and Xoff characters |
| `XonLimit` | Parameter for software flow control. Specifies the minimum number of bytes allowed in the input buffer before the XonChar is sent. |
| `XoffLimit` | Parameter for software flow control. Specifies the maximum number of bytes in the input buffer before the XoffChar is sent. The maximum number of bytes allowed is calculated by subtracting this value from the size, in bytes, of the input buffer. Note XonLimit and XoffLimit must be set properly to prevent errors, i.e. XonLimit < (input buffer size – XoffLimit) must apply. |
| `XonChar` | Parameter for software flow control. It specifies the value of the XON character to start an operation (transmission as well as reception). |
| `XoffChar` | Parameter for software flow control. It specifies the value of the XOFF character to suspend an operation (transmission as well as reception). Note The transmitted data must not contain the XON and XOFF characters if software flow control is used. XON and XOFF are sent only once for each transition. |


**Return Value:** 0 error The error occurs if 1 success


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```


---

### RS232Send

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232Send.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232Send.htm)

**Description:**

Sends a block of bytes to a serial port.


**Syntax:**

```c
dword RS232Send( dword port, byte buffer[], dword number )
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `port` | A number between 1 and 255 identifying a serial port. |
| `buffer` | An array of bytes of which number will be sent. |
| `number` | Number of bytes to send. |


**Return Value:** 0 error The error occurs if An error is only given if a problem is issued directly. To get informed about errors occurring in later stages of the operation use RS232OnError . 1 success The operation may fail in later stages. Success means here, that the send operation could be started properly.


**Example(s):**

```c
char text[20] = "Hello World !"; byte buffer[20]; int i; int length; length=strlen(text)+1; for (i=0;i<length;i++) buffer[i]=text[i]; if ( 1==RS232Send(1,buffer,length) ) write("It works with port 1.");
```


---

### RS232Receive

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232Receive.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232Receive.htm)

**Description:**

Receive blocks of bytes from a serial port.


**Syntax:**

```c
dword RS232Receive( dword port, byte buffer[], dword size );
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `port` | A number between 1 and 255 identifying a serial port. |
| `buffer` | An array of bytes. |
| `size` | Maximum number of bytes which can be received. |


**Return Value:** 0 error The error occurs if An error is only given if a problem is issued directly by the system. To get informed about errors occurring in later stages of the operation use RS232OnError . 1 success Success means here, that the receive operation could be started properly.


**Example(s):**

```c
byte mybuffer[20]; int mysize=20; if ( 1==RS232Receive(1,mybuffer,mysize) ) write("It works with port 1."); ... RS232OnReceive( dword port, dword buffer[], dword number ) { // works after first RS232Receive ! // buffer == mybuffer, 1<number<=mysize }
```


---

### RS232SetSignalLine

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232SetSignalLine.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232SetSignalLine.htm)

**Description:**

Sets signal lines on serial port. Note If you use hardware handshake, then this function may conflict with the automatic handshaking.


**Syntax:**

```c
dword RS232SetSignalLine( dword port, dword line, dword state );
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `port` | A number between 1 and 255 identifying a serial port. |
| `line` | Specifies signal line of which the state shall be set. 0 RTS 1 DTR Data-Terminal-Ready (from sender). RTS: Request-To-Send (from sender). |
| `0` | RTS |
| `1` | DTR |
| `state` | 0 disabled 1 enabled |


**Return Value:** 0 error The error occurs if 1 success


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```


---

### RS232OnSend

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232OnSend.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232OnSend.htm)

**Description:**

Callback handler for completion of send operation to a serial port. Note Will be called only at the node which issued the send operation. The handler will only be called for success. For errors RS232OnError will be called.


**Syntax:**

```c
RS232OnSend( dword port, byte buffer[], dword number );
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `port` | A number between 1 and 255 identifying a serial port. |
| `buffer` | The buffer given to the send call. |
| `number` | The number of bytes which have been sent. |


**Return Value:** 0 error The error occurs if no send operation will be used. 1 success


**Example(s):**

```c
char text[20] = "Hello World !"; byte block[20]; int i; int length; length=strlen(text)+1; for (i=0;i<length;i++) block[i]=text[i]; if ( 0!=RS232Send(1,block,length) ) write("Written block of bytes to port 1."); ... // at node which issued the send request RS232OnSend(dword port, byte buffer[], dword number) { // send completed now, it may time to issue the next send // buffer==block, number==length }
```


---

### RS232OnReceive

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232OnReceive.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232OnReceive.htm)

**Description:**

Callback handler for reception of data at a serial port. Note Will be called only at the nodes which issued a RS232Receive operation for the given port. The handler will only be called for success. For errors RS232OnError will be called. The buffer may be much larger than number. For very slow connections, the handler may get called for few bytes. At least one byte is given. It is possible to listen to one and the same port at several nodes. All nodes will be called with the same data.


**Syntax:**

```c
RS232OnReceive( dword port, byte buffer[], dword number );
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `port` | A number between 1 and 255 identifying a serial port. |
| `buffer` | The buffer given to the start receive call . |
| `number` | The number of bytes which have been received. Note The number of received bytes will vary strongly with the speed of the connection. |


**Return Value:** 0 error The error occurs if no start receive operation has been. 1 success


**Example(s):**

```c
// at sender node char text[20] = "Hello World !"; byte block[20]; int i; int length; length=strlen(text)+1; for (i=0;i<length;i++) block[i]=text[i]; if ( 0!=RS232Send(1,block,length) ) write("Written block of bytes to port 1."); ... // at receiver node (here 2) byte mybuffer[100]; int mysize = 100; RS232Receive(2,mybuffer,mysize); ... // at node which issued the receive request RS232OnReceive(dword port, byte buffer[], dword number) { // port==2 here // buffer==mybuffer, number<=mysize // buffer contains some parts of block }
```


---

### RS232OnError

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232OnError.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/Functions/CAPLfunctionRS232OnError.htm)

**Description:**

Callback handler for reception of errors at a serial port. Note Will be called only at the node which issued send/receive operations. For repetitive errors (stable error condition) just the first one is returned. That case may happen for reception errors above all, i.e. at configuration mismatches of both connected ends. It is possible that in case of configuration mismatches errors may not occur at once, e.g. a configuration of 9600/8/1/no parity at one port and 115200/8/1/no parity at the other end may seem to work (though rubbish is received).


**Syntax:**

```c
RS232OnError( dword port, dword errorFlags );
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `port` | A number between 1 and 255 identifying a serial port. |
| `errorFlags` | Cumulative summary of what went wrong. Bits are set to flag conditions. Bit Error 0 Send operation failed. 1 Receive operation failed. 2 Frame error. May be caused by  parity mismatch or any other frame mismatch (e.g. number of stop bits). 3 Frame parity error. Is caused by parity mismatch. 4 Buffer overrun. It is not specified if the driver of the sender cannot send fast enough, if it is up to the receiver which got too much data in too short time or anything else. 5 Buffer overrun at receiver. 6 Break state. Other end requested to pause. 7 Timeout. May be caused by wrongly set too short timeout. See RS232SetHandshake for setting the timeout. Note Several error bits may be set at the same time. Some error flags are up to the driver manufacturer what they mean and when they are issued. |
| `Bit` | Error |
| `0` | Send operation failed. |
| `1` | Receive operation failed. |
| `2` | Frame error. May be caused by  parity mismatch or any other frame mismatch (e.g. number of stop bits). |
| `3` | Frame parity error. Is caused by parity mismatch. |
| `4` | Buffer overrun. It is not specified if the driver of the sender cannot send fast enough, if it is up to the receiver which got too much data in too short time or anything else. |
| `5` | Buffer overrun at receiver. |
| `6` | Break state. Other end requested to pause. |
| `7` | Timeout. May be caused by wrongly set too short timeout. See RS232SetHandshake for setting the timeout. |


**Example(s):**

```c
RS232OnError(dword port, dword errorFlags) { if ( errorFlags & 1 ) writeLineEx(0,3,"send failed"); if ( errorFlags & 2 ) writeLineEx(0,3,"receive failed"); }
```


---

### RS232 CAPL Functions: Deprecated INI File

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/CAPLfunctionsRS232DeprecatedIniFiles.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/CAPLfunctionsRS232DeprecatedIniFiles.htm)

### RS232 API

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CANoeCANalyzer/Interfaces/RS232/RS232.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CANoeCANalyzer/Interfaces/RS232/RS232.htm)


---

### RS232 CAPL Functions

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/CAPLfunctionsRS232Overview.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/RS232/CAPLfunctionsRS232Overview.htm)


## Signal Access {#signal-access}


---

### GetGeneralSignalType

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SignalAccess/CAPLfunctionGetGeneralSignalType.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SignalAccess/CAPLfunctionGetGeneralSignalType.htm)

**Description:**

Determines the general type of a signal, i.e., whether it is a database signal, a service signal, etc.


**Syntax:**

```c
long GetGeneralSignalType (char signalName[])
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `signalName` | The qualified name of the signal. The syntax is [Channel::][Database name (alias)::][Node::][Message::]Signa; the order and completeness of the objects from right to left is important. See the example for getSignal . |


**Return Value:** 0 Signal was not found. 1 Signal is a database signal. 2 Signal is a service signal.


**Example(s):**

```c
long SetGenericSignalValue(char signalName[], double value) { signal * sig; serviceSignal * serviceSig; switch (GetGeneralSignalType(signalName)) { case 1: sig = LookupSignal(signalName); $sig = value; return 1; case 2: serviceSig = LookupServiceSignal(signalName); $serviceSig = value; return 1; default: return 0; } }
```


---

### getSignalDescriptionForValue

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SignalAccess/CAPLfunctionGetSignalDescriptionForValue.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SignalAccess/CAPLfunctionGetSignalDescriptionForValue.htm)

**Description:**

Retrieves the description for the value of a database signal. In this way, you can access the value table of the signal.


**Syntax:**

```c
long getSignalDescriptionForValue(Signal  signal, int64 rawValue, char buffer[], long bufferSize); // form 1
```

```c
long getSignalDescriptionForValue(char signalNamel[], int64 rawValue, char buffer[], long bufferSize); // form 2
```

```c
long getSignalDescriptionForValue(serviceSignalNumber signal, int64 rawValue, char buffer[], long bufferSize); // form 3
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `signal` | The signal (form 1). |
| `rawValue` | The value for which the description shall be retrieved. This must be the raw value, not the physical value. |
| `buffer` | String buffer which receives the value description. |
| `bufferSize` | Size of the buffer. |
| `signalName` | The name of the signal (form 2). Note Signal Ambiguity You have to use further objects to identify the signal uniquely. They are: channel, database name (alias), node name and message name. The exact qualification syntax is: [Channel::][Database name (alias)::][Node::][Message::]Signal The order and completeness of the objects from right to left is important  (see example). |


**Return Value:** 0 No error, function successful. -1 The signal is not valid. -2 There is no value table for the signal or the value table doesn't contain the value. -3 The provided buffer is too small. -4 The description contains special characters which can't be represented in the current CAPL string encoding. -5 The signal was not found (form 2 only).


**Example(s):**

```c
char buffer[100]; long ret; ret = getSignalDescriptionForValue(PowerTrain::Engine::GearBoxInfo::Gear, 1, buffer, elcount(buffer)); if (ret == 0) write("Description for value 1 is '%s'", buffer); else write("Error getting description for value 1: %d", ret);
```


---

### getSignalValueForDescription

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SignalAccess/CAPLfunctionGetSignalValueForDescription.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SignalAccess/CAPLfunctionGetSignalValueForDescription.htm)

**Description:**

Retrieves the value for a value description of a database signal. In this way, you can access the value table of the signal.


**Syntax:**

```c
long getSignalValueForDescription(Signal  signal, char description[], int64& rawValue); // form 1
```

```c
long getSignalValueForDescription(char signalNamel[], char description[], int64& rawValue); // form 2
```

```c
long getSignalValueForDescription(serviceSignalNumber signal, char description[], int64& rawValue); // form 3
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `signal` | The signal (form 1). |
| `description` | The value description for which the value shall be retrieved. |
| `rawValue` | The value for the description. This is the raw value, not the physical value. |
| `signalName` | The name of the signal (form 2). Note Signal Ambiguity You have to use further objects to identify the signal uniquely. They are: channel, database name (alias), node name and message name. The exact qualification syntax is: [Channel::][Database name (alias)::][Node::][Message::]Signal The order and completeness of the objects from right to left is important  (see example). |


**Return Value:** 0 No error, function successful. -1 The signal is not valid. -2 There is no value table for the signal or the value table doesn't contain the description. -4 The description contains special characters which can't be represented on the current computer. -5 The signal was not found (form 2 only).


**Example(s):**

```c
int64 val; long ret; ret = getSignalValueForDescription(PowerTrain::Engine::GearBoxInfo::Gear, "Gear_2", val); if (ret == 0) write("Value for description 'Gear_2' is %I64d", val); else write("Error getting value for description 'Gear_2': %d", ret);
```


---

### ReadByteArraySignalAsInteger

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SignalAccess/CAPLfunctionReadByteArraySignalAsInteger.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SignalAccess/CAPLfunctionReadByteArraySignalAsInteger.htm)

**Description:**

Reads the value of a byte array signal and treats it as a 64-bit integer. Use this function if you previously read the value of a byte array signal with the .raw64 selector. This selector works only for integer signals, but byte array signals were treated incorrectly as integer signals in CANoe versions < 12.0 SP3.


**Syntax:**

```c
dword ReadByteArraySignalAsInteger(signal aSignal, qword& value, dword direction, dword byteOrder, dword byteFillMode) // form 1
```

```c
dword ReadByteArraySignalAsInteger(pdu aPDU, char signalName[], qword& value, dword byteOrder, dword byteFillMode) // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `aSignal` | A byte array signal |
| `aPDU` | An AUTOSAR PDU with a byte array signal |
| `signalName` | Name of the signal on the PDU |
| `value` | The read value (out parameter) |
| `direction` | Whether the rx signal (parameter value 0) or the tx signal value buffer (parameter value 1) shall be read. |
| `byteOrder` | Byte order for interpreting the signal bytes. 0: little-endian (intel) 1: big-endian (motorola) See examples below. |
| `byteFillMode` | How the remaining bits of the 64-bit integer shall be filled if the signal has less than 64 bits. 0 means add 0 bits before the signal bits. 1: sign-extend. Add 0 or 1 bits before the signal bits, depending on the first signal bit. 2: Add 0 bits after the signal bits (effectively a right-shift of the value). See examples below. |


**Return Value:** 0 No error 1 The signal has more than 64 bits. -2 The signal is not a byte array. 3 Signal not found on the PDU (form 2).


**Example(s):**

```c
byteFillMode == 0: value == 0x009123456789ABCD // prepend 0s byteFillMode == 1: value == 0xFF9123456789ABCD // sign-extension byteFillMode == 2: value == 0x9123456789abcd00 // append 0s
byteFillMode == 0: value == 0x0023456789ABCDEF // prepend 0s byteFillMode == 1: value == 0x0023456789ABCDEF // sign-extension byteFillMode == 2: value == 0x23456789ABCDEF00 // append 0s
```

> If the value shall be interpreted in intel (little-endian) byte order, depending on the byteFillMode parameter, the following value will be read if the bytes of the signal are "EF CD AB 89 67 45 23":


---

### resetSignalValues

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SignalAccess/CAPLfunctionResetSignalValues.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SignalAccess/CAPLfunctionResetSignalValues.htm)

**Description:**

Function to reset signals to their initial values. Depending on the mode parameter, the signals may be reset either to their initial value as configured in the database or to their start value as configured in der Start Values dialog.


**Syntax:**

```c
void resetSignalValues(dword mode); // form 1
```

```c
void resetSignalValues(dword mode, eBusType busType, dword channelNumber); // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `mode` | Reset signals either to their initial value or to their start value. Values: 0: Reset signals to their initial database value 1: Reset signals to their start value |
| `busType` | Reset only the signals of the specified bus type. |
| `channelNumber` | Reset only the signals of the specified channel number. |


**Example(s):**

```c
/** * Reset all signals. * * @param mode Reset signals to their initial value (0) or to their start value (1). */ void resetSignalValues(dword mode); /** * Reset signals of the specified application channel. * * @param mode Reset signals to their initial value (0) or to their start value (1). * @param busType Bus type for which the signals shall be resetted. * @param channelNumber Channel number for which the signals shall be resetted. */ void resetSignalValues(dword mode, eBusType busType, dword channelNumber);
```


---

### WriteByteArraySignalAsInteger

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SignalAccess/CAPLfunctionWriteByteArraySignalAsInteger.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SignalAccess/CAPLfunctionWriteByteArraySignalAsInteger.htm)

**Description:**

Writes the value of a byte array signal, treating it as a 64-bit integer. Use this function if you previously wrote the value of a byte array signal with the .raw64 selector. This selector works only for integer signals, but byte array signals were treated incorrectly as integer signals in CANoe versions < 12.0 SP3.


**Syntax:**

```c
dword WriteByteArraySignalAsInteger(signal aSignal, qword value, dword byteOrder, dword byteCutMode) // form 1
```

```c
dword WriteByteArraySignalAsInteger(pdu aPDU, char signalName[], qword value, dword byteOrder, dword byteCutMode) // form2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `aSignal` | A byte array signal |
| `aPDU` | An AUTOSAR PDU with a byte array signal |
| `signalName` | Name of the signal on the PDU |
| `value` | The to be written value. |
| `byteOrder` | Byte order for interpreting the signal bytes. 0: little-endian (intel) 1: big-endian (motorola) See examples below. |
| `byteCutMode` | How the additional bits of the 64-bit value shall be cut if the signal has less than 64 bits. 0: ignore bits a the end 1: ignore bits at the beginning See examples below. |


**Return Value:** 0 No error 1 The signal has more than 64 bits. -2 The signal is not a byte array. 3 Signal not found on the PDU (form 2).


**Example(s):**

```c
byteCutMode == 0: 91 23 45 67 89 AB CD byteCutMode == 1: 23 45 67 89 AB CD EF
byteCutMode == 0: EF CD AB 89 67 45 23 byteCutMode == 1: CD AB 89 67 45 23 91
```

> If the value shall be written in intel (little-endian) byte order, depending on the byteCutMode parameter, the following bytes will be set in the signal:


## Struct Byte Access {#struct-byte-access}


---

### memcmp

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/StructByteAccess/Functions/CAPLfunctionMemCmp.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/StructByteAccess/Functions/CAPLfunctionMemCmp.htm)

**Description:**

Compares the bytes of the parameters. In form 3, both structs must have the same type. Forms 5-13 compare vCDL objects with themselves or byte arrays. In form 5-7, dest and source must habe the same type. In form 8-13, the vCDL object must have a fixed layout.


**Syntax:**

```c
int memcmp(struct * dest, byte source[]); // form 1
```

```c
int memcmp(byte dest[], struct * source); // form 2
```

```c
int memcmp(struct * dest, struct * source); // form 3
```

```c
int memcmp(byte dest[], byte source[], dword size); // form 4
```

```c
int memcmp(struct dest, struct source); // form 5
```

```c
int memcmp(array dest, array source); // form 6
```

```c
int memcmp(union dest, union source); // form 7
```

```c
int memcmp(struct dest, byte source[]); // form 8
```

```c
int memcmp(array dest, byte source[]); // form 9
```

```c
int memcmp(union dest, byte source[]); // form 10
```

```c
int memcmp(byte dest[], struct source); // form 11
```

```c
int memcmp(byte dest[], array source); // form 12
```

```c
int memcmp(byte dest[], union source); // form 13
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `dest` | A struct / byte array / vCDL object |
| `source` | Another struct / byte array / vCDL object |
| `size` | Size of the arrays (number of bytes to compare). |


**Return Value:** 0 if the bytes are equal; a value different from 0 if they are unequal


**Example(s):**

```c
byte data[4]; struct WrapDword { dword dw; } dwordWrapper; int i; for (i = 0; i < elcount(data); ++i) data[i] = i; dwordWrapper.dw = 0x03020100; if (memcmp(dwordWrapper, data) == 0) write("Data represents the number: Little Endian is used.");
```


---

### memcpy

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/StructByteAccess/Functions/CAPLfunctionMemCpy.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/StructByteAccess/Functions/CAPLfunctionMemCpy.htm)

**Description:**

Copies bytes from a source to a destination. In form 9, both structs must have the same type. In other forms with structs, the arrays must be large enough to contain the struct data. In forms 18 to 22, the payload size and the struct size must be identical.


**Syntax:**

```c
void memcpy(byte dest[], struct * source); // form 1
```

```c
void memcpy(char dest[], struct * source); // form 2
```

```c
void memcpy(byte dest[], long offset, struct * source); // form 3
```

```c
void memcpy(char dest[], long offset, struct * source); // form 4
```

```c
void memcpy(struct * dest, byte source[]); // form 5
```

```c
void memcpy(struct * dest, char source[]); // form 6
```

```c
void memcpy(struct * dest, byte source[], long offset); // form 7
```

```c
void memcpy(struct * dest, char source[], long offset); // form 8
```

```c
void memcpy(struct * dest, struct * source); // form 9
```

```c
void memcpy(byte dest[], byte source[], dword length); // form 10
```

```c
void memcpy(byte dest[], char source[], dword length); // form 11
```

```c
void memcpy(char dest[], byte source[], dword length); // form 12
```

```c
void memcpy(char dest[], char source[], dword length); // form 13
```

```c
void memcpy(struct dest, char source[]); // form 14
```

```c
void memcpy(struct dest, byte source[]); // form 15
```

```c
void memcpy(char dest[], struct source); // form 16
```

```c
void memcpy(byte dest[], struct source); // form 17
```

```c
void memcpy(PDUPayload dest, PDUPayload source, dword length); // form 18
```

```c
void memcpy(PDUPayload dest, byte source[], dword length); // form 19
```

```c
void memcpy(byte source[], PDUPayload dest, dword length); // form 20
```

```c
void memcpy(PDUPayload dest, struct * source); // form 21
```

```c
void memcpy(struct * dest, PDUPayload source); // form 22
```

```c
void memcpy(bytes dest, byte source[]); // form 23
```

```c
void memcpy(bytes dest, char source[]); // form 24
```

```c
void memcpy(bytes dest, byte source[], dword length); // form 25
```

```c
void memcpy(bytes dest, char source[], dword length); // form 26
```

```c
void memcpy(byte dest[], bytes source); // form 27
```

```c
void memcpy(char dest[], bytes source); // form 28
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `source` | (form 1-4, 9, 16, 17, 21): Struct whose bytes shall be copied. (form 18, 22): Payload data of a PDU whose bytes shall be copied. (form 27, 28): vCDL "bytes" value that shall be copied. (other forms): Array whose bytes shall be copied. |
| `dest` | (form 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 22): Struct into which the bytes shall be copied. (form 18, 19, 20, 21): Payload data of a PDU into which the bytes shall be copied. (form 23, 24, 25, 26): vCDL "bytes" value into which the bytes shall be copied. (other forms): Array into which the bytes shall be copied. |
| `offset` | (form 3, 4, 7, 8): Offset in the array which marks the start of the data. |
| `length` | (form 10, 11, 12, 13, 18, 19, 20, 25, 26): number of bytes which shall be copied. |


**Example(s):**

```c
byte data[4]; struct WrapDword { dword dw; } dwordWrapper; int i; for (i = 0; i < elcount(data); ++i) data[i] = i; memcpy(dwordWrapper, data); write("Bytes as dword: %0#10lx", dwordWrapper.dw); dwordWrapper.dw = 0x12345678; memcpy(data, dwordWrapper); write("dword as bytes: %#lx %#lx %#lx %#lx", data[0], data[1], data[2], data[3]);
```


---

### memcpy_h2n

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/StructByteAccess/Functions/CAPLfunctionMemCpyH2n.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/StructByteAccess/Functions/CAPLfunctionMemCpyH2n.htm)

**Description:**

Copies the bytes from the struct into the array, and translates the byte order of the elements from little-endian to big-endian (h2n stands for "host to network").


**Syntax:**

```c
void memcpy_h2n(byte dest[], struct source); // form 1
```

```c
void memcpy_h2n(byte dest[], int offset, struct source); // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `source` | Struct whose bytes shall be copied |
| `dest` | Array into which the bytes shall be copied |
| `offset (form 2)` | Offset into the array |


**Example(s):**

```c
byte data[4]; struct WrapDword { dword dw; } dwordWrapper; int i; for (i = 0; i < elcount(data); ++i) data[i] = i; memcpy_n2h(dwordWrapper, data); write("Bytes as dword: %0#10lx", dwordWrapper.dw); dwordWrapper.dw = 0x12345678; memcpy_h2n(data, dwordWrapper); write("dword as bytes: %#lx %#lx %#lx %#lx", data[0], data[1], data[2], data[3]);
```


---

### memcpy_n2h

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/StructByteAccess/Functions/CAPLfunctionMemCpyN2h.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/StructByteAccess/Functions/CAPLfunctionMemCpyN2h.htm)

**Description:**

Fills the struct with bytes from the array, and translates the byte order of the elements from big-endian to little-endian (n2h stands for "network to host").


**Syntax:**

```c
void memcpy_n2h(struct dest, byte source[]); // form 1
```

```c
void memcpy_n2h(struct dest, byte source[], int offset); // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `source` | Array whose bytes shall be copied |
| `dest` | Struct into which the bytes shall be copied |
| `offset (form 2)` | Offset in the array where the data begins |


---

### memcpy_off

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/StructByteAccess/Functions/CAPLfunctionMemCpy_off.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/StructByteAccess/Functions/CAPLfunctionMemCpy_off.htm)

**Description:**

Copies bytes from a source to destination, giving a destination start offset. The size of the destination must be at least destOffset + length.


**Syntax:**

```c
void memcpy_off( struct type dest, dword destOffset, byte source[], dword sourceOffset, dword length); // form 1
```

```c
void memcpy_off( struct type dest, dword destOffset, char source[], dword sourceOffset, dword length); // form 2
```

```c
void memcpy_off( byte dest[], dword destOffset, struct type source, dword sourceOffset, dword length); // form 3
```

```c
void memcpy_off( char dest[], dword destOffset, struct type source, dword sourceOffset, dword length); // form 4
```

```c
void memcpy_off( byte dest[], dword destOffset, byte source[], dword sourceOffset, dword length); // form 5
```

```c
void memcpy_off( char dest[], dword destOffset, byte source[], dword sourceOffset, dword length); // form 6
```

```c
void memcpy_off( byte dest[], dword destOffset, char source[], dword sourceOffset, dword length); // form 7
```

```c
void memcpy_off( char dest[], dword destOffset, char source[], dword sourceOffset, dword length); // form 8
```

```c
void memcpy_off( struct dest, dword destOffset, char source[], dword sourceOffset, dword length); // form 9
```

```c
void memcpy_off( union dest, dword destOffset, char source[], dword sourceOffset, dword length); // form 10
```

```c
void memcpy_off( array dest, dword destOffset, char source[], dword sourceOffset, dword length); // form 11
```

```c
void memcpy_off( byte[] dest, dword destOffset, struct source, dword sourceOffset, dword length); // form 12
```

```c
void memcpy_off( byte[] dest, dword destOffset, union source, dword sourceOffset, dword length); // form 13
```

```c
void memcpy_off( byte[] dest, dword destOffset, array source, dword sourceOffset, dword length); // form 14
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `dest` | (form 1, 2): Struct into which the bytes shall be copied. (form, 9, 10, 11): vCDL object into which the bytes shall be copied. (other forms): Array into which the bytes shall be copied. |
| `source` | (form 3, 4): Struct from which the bytes shall be copied. (form 12, 13, 14): vCDL object from which the bytes shall be copied. (other forms): Array from which the bytes shall be copied. |
| `destOffset` | Start offset in the destination struct or array. |
| `sourceOffset` | Start offset int the source struct or array. |
| `length` | Number of bytes which shall be copied. |


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```

### Struct Byte Access CAPL Functions

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/StructByteAccess/CAPLfunctionsStructByteAccessOverview.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/StructByteAccess/CAPLfunctionsStructByteAccessOverview.htm)


## System Variables {#system-variables}


---

### Direct Access to Values from System Variables

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/Shared/CAPL/SignalOrientedProgramming/SOPAccessSystemVariable.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/Shared/CAPL/SignalOrientedProgramming/SOPAccessSystemVariable.htm)


---

### sysDefineVariableData

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysDefineVariableData.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysDefineVariableData.htm)

**Description:**

Defines a variable of data type.


**Syntax:**

```c
long sysDefineVariableData(char namespace[], char variable[], byte initialData[], long initialSize);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `namespace` | Name of the namespace. |
| `variable` | Name of the variable. |
| `initialData` | Initial value of the variable. |
| `initialSize` | Initial size (in bytes) of the variable. Must not exceed the length of the initialData array. |


**Return Value:** 0 no error, function successful 1 namespace was not found or second try to define the same namespace 2 variable was not found or second try to define the same variable 3 no writing right for the namespace available 4 the variable has no suitable type for the function


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```


---

### sysDefineVariableFloat

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysDefineVariableFloat.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysDefineVariableFloat.htm)

**Description:**

Defines a variable of the float type.


**Syntax:**

```c
long sysDefineVariableFloat(char namespace[], char variable[], double initialValue); // form 1
```

```c
long sysDefineVariableFloat(char namespace[], char variable[], double initialValue, double minimum, double maximum); // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `namespace` | Name of the namespace. |
| `variable` | Name of the variable. |
| `initialValue` | Initial value of the variable. |
| `minimum` | The minimum value of the variable. |
| `maximum` | The maximum value of the variable. |


**Return Value:** 0 no error, function successful 1 namespace was not found or second try to define the same namespace 2 variable was not found or second try to define the same variable 3 no writing right for the namespace available 4 the variable has no suitable type for the function 5 given minimum is larger than the given maximum


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```


---

### sysDefineVariableFloatArray

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysDefineVariableFloatArray.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysDefineVariableFloatArray.htm)

**Description:**

Defines a variable of the float[] type.


**Syntax:**

```c
long sysDefineVariableFloatArray(char namespace[], char vari-able[], float initialValues[], long arraySize); // form 1
```

```c
long sysDefineVariableFloatArray(char namespace[], char variable[], double initialValues[], long arraySize, double minimum, double maximum); // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `namespace` | Name of the namespace. |
| `variable` | Name of the variable. |
| `initialValue` | Initial value of the variable. |
| `arraySize` | Number of values in the array. |
| `minimum` | The minimum value of the variable. |
| `maximum` | The maximum value of the variable. |


**Return Value:** 0 no error, function successful 1 namespace was not found or second try to define the same namespace 2 variable was not found or second try to define the same variable 3 no writing right for the namespace available 4 the variable has no suitable type for the function 5 given minimum is larger than the given maximum


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```

### sysGetOrigTimeNS

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetOrigTimeNS.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetOrigTimeNS.htm)

**Description:**

Returns the original time stamp of the last update to the variable value, before time synchronization may have changed it.


**Syntax:**

```c
int64 sysGetOrigTimeNS(SysVarName);
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `SysVarName` | Name of the fully qualified name of the system variable, including all namespaces, separated by "::". The name must be preceded by "sysVar::". |


**Return Value:** Original time stamp of the last update to the variable value in nanoseconds since measurement start.


**Example(s):**

```c
int64 timeStamp; timeStamp = sysGetOrigTimeNS(sysvar::MyNamespace::FloatVar);
```


---

### sysGetVariableArrayLength

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableArrayLength.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableArrayLength.htm)

**Description:**

Returns the length of the array of the system variable. Note


**Syntax:**

```c
dword sysGetVariableArrayLength(char namespace[], char variable[]); // form 1
```

```c
dword sysGetVariableArrayLength(SysVarName); // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `namespace` | Name of the namespace. |
| `variable` | Name of the variable. |
| `SysVarName` | Name of the fully qualified name of the system variable, including all namespaces, separated by "::". The name must be preceded by "sysVar::". |


**Return Value:** For system variables of type data, returns the current size (in bytes) of the value. For system variables of type long or float array, returns the number of elements in the array. For system variables of type string, returns the length of the string, excluding the terminating 0 character. For system variables of type long or float, returns 1.


**Example(s):**

```c
// calculate the norm of a vector dword length, i; double element, sum, norm; sum = 0.0; length = sysGetVariableArrayLength(sysvar::MyVariables::MyVector); for (i = 0; i < length; ++i) { element = @sysvar::MyVariables::MyVector[i]; sum += element * element; } norm = sqrt(sum);
```


---

### sysGetVariableArraySize

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableArraySize.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableArraySize.htm)

**Description:**

Retrieves the current array size of a system variable which has a generic array data type. Note


**Syntax:**

```c
dword sysGetVariableArraySize(sysvar variable) // form 1
```

```c
long sysGetVariableArraySize(char namespace[], char variable[], dword& arraySize) // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `variable (form 1)` | Name of the fully qualified name of the system variable, including all namespaces, separated by "::". The name must be preceded by "sysVar::". |
| `namespace (form 2)` | Name of the namespace |
| `variable (form 2)` | Name of the variable |
| `arraySize (form 2)` | current size of the array (out-parameter) |


**Return Value:** Form 1 current size of the array Form 2 error code: 0 – no error 2 – variable not found 4 – variable is not of a generic array type


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```

### sysGetVariableData

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableData.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableData.htm)

**Description:**

Returns the value of a variable of the type data, string, struct or generic array. Note


**Syntax:**

```c
long sysGetVariableData(char namespace[], char variable[], byte buffer[], long& copiedBytes); // form 1
```

```c
long sysGetVariableData(SysVarName, byte buffer[], long& copiedBytes); // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `namespace` | Name of the namespace. |
| `variable` | Name of the variable. |
| `buffer` | Buffer which takes the value of the variable. |
| `copiedBytes` | Receives the number of bytes which were copied into the buffer. If the buffer is too small, no bytes will be copied; else the parameter will contain the current size of the variable. |
| `SysVarName` | Name of the fully qualified name of the system variable, including all namespaces, separated by "::". The name must be preceded by "sysVar::". |


**Return Value:** 0 no error, function successful 1 namespace was not found or second try to define the same namespace 2 variable was not found or second try to define the same variable 3 no writing right for the namespace available 4 the variable has no suitable type for the function 5 the buffer is too small for the value


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```

### sysGetVariableDescriptionForValue

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableDescriptionForValue.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableDescriptionForValue.htm)

**Description:**

Retrieves a description for a value of a system variable of type long or long array. In this way, you can access the value table of the system variable.


**Syntax:**

```c
long sysGetVariableDescriptionForValue(SysVarName, long value, char buffer[], long bufferSize); // form 1
```

```c
long sysGetVariableDescriptionForValue(char namespace[], char variable[], long value, char buffer[], long bufferSize); // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `namespace` | Name of the namespace. |
| `variable` | Name of the variable. |
| `value` | Value for which the description shall be retrieved. |
| `buffer` | Buffer which receives the description. |
| `bufferSize` | Size of the buffer. |
| `SysVarName` | Name of the fully qualified name of the system variable, including all namespaces, separated by "::". The name must be preceded by "sysVar::". |


**Return Value:** 0 no error, function successful 2 variable was not found or second try to define the same variable 5 there is no description of this value for the variable 6 the buffer is not large enough for the description


**Example(s):**

```c
char buffer[20]; sysGetVariableDescriptionForValue("Dynamic", "IntVar", 0, buffer, elcount(buffer));
```

### sysGetVariableDWord

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableDWord.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableDWord.htm)

**Description:**

Returns the value of a variable of type unsigned 32 bit integer. Note


**Syntax:**

```c
dword sysGetVariableDWord(char namespace[], char variable[]); // form 1
```

```c
dword sysGetVariableDWord(SysVarName); // form 2
```

```c
long sysGetVariableDWord(char namespace[], char variable[], dword& value); // form 3
```

```c
long sysGetVariableDWord(SysVarName, dword& value); // form 4
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `namespace` | Name of the namespace. |
| `variable` | Name of the variable. |
| `value` | Receives the current value of the variable. |
| `SysVarName` | Name of the fully qualified name of the system variable, including all namespaces, separated by "::". The name must be preceded by "sysVar::". |


**Return Value:** Value of the variable or 0 in case of error (form 1 and 2) 0 no error, function successful (form 3 and 4) 2 variable was not found or second try to define the same variable (form 3 and 4) 4 the variable has no suitable type for the function (form 3 and 4)


**Example(s):**

```c
sysGetVariableDWord(sysvar::MyNamespace::UInt32Var);
```

### sysGetVariableFloat

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableFloat.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableFloat.htm)

**Description:**

Returns the value of a variable of the double type. Note


**Syntax:**

```c
double sysGetVariableFloat(char namespace[], char variable[]); // form 1
```

```c
double sysGetVariableFloat(SysVarName); // form 2
```

```c
long sysGetVariableFloat(char namespace[], char variable[], double& value); // form 3
```

```c
long sysGetVariableFloat(SysVarName, double& value); // form 4
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `namespace` | Name of the namespace (form 1). |
| `variable` | Name of the variable (form 1). |
| `SysVarName` | Name of the fully qualified name of the system variable, including all namespaces, separated by "::". The name must be preceded by "sysVar::". |
| `value` | Receives the current value of the variable. |


**Return Value:** Value of the variable or 0 in case of error (form 1 and 2) 0 No error, function successful (form 3 and 4) 2 Variable was not found (form 3 and 4) 4 Variable has no suitable type for the function (form 3 and 4)


**Example(s):**

*Example 1*

> The value of the FloatVar system variable will be determined an displayed in the Write Window. on key 'b' { double value; value = SysGetVariableFloat(sysvar::MyNamespace::FloatVar); write("Value of FloatVar is %g", value); }

*Example 2*

> This example shows the use of system variable types as a return value of functions and as a local variable. The user-defined CAPL function getSysVarFloat returns any system variable of type Float . The function is therefore defined with the system variable type sysvarFloat as the return value. The system variable sv1 (defined with system variable type Float ) represents any system variable depending on the counter of the getSysVarFloat function. The value of a system variable is set with the CAPL function sysSetVariableFloat . The value of the system variable is get with the CAPL function sysGetVariableFloat and output in the Write Window.

*Note*

```c
on key 'c' { sysvarFloat * svFloat1;  //defines a local system variable 'sv1Float' with the system variable type 'sysvarFloat' float valueSysVar; svFloat1 = getSysVarFloat(0); //@svFloat1 = 1.5; write("Variable is %s, Value is %7.3f", svFloat1.name, @svFloat1); //@svFloat1 = @svFloat1*2.5; sysSetVariableFloat(svFloat1, @svFloat1*2.5); write("Variable is %s, Value is higher: %7.3f", svFloat1.name, @svFloat1); svFloat1 = getSysVarFloat(1); write("Variable is %s, Value is %7.3f", svFloat1.name, @svFloat1); svFloat1 = getSysVarFloat(2); valueSysVar = sysGetVariableFloat(svFloat1); write("Variable is %s, Value is %d", svFloat1.name, valueSysVar); } sysvarFloat * getSysVarFloat(int cKey) { switch (cKey) { case 0: return sysvar::FMW1::KeyFloat; case 1: return sysvar::DCM::SpeedSignalFloat; default: return sysvar::Engine::EngineFloat; } }
```

> You must define the system variables FMW1::KeyFloat , DCM::SpeedSignalFloat , and Engine::EngineFloat in the System Variables Configuration dialog first.


---

### sysGetVariableFloatArray

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableFloatArray.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableFloatArray.htm)

**Description:**

Returns the value of a variable of the float[] type. If the actual array size of the system variable is larger than the array size given as parameter, all elements in the values array up to the array size given as parameter will receive the current values of the system variable. Elements in the values array beyond the array size given as parameter will remain unchanged. If the actual array size of the system variable is smaller than the array size given as parameter, additional elements in the values array will be set to 0. Elements in the values array beyond the array size given as parameter will remain unchanged. Never give as parameter an array size which is larger than real size of the values array, this would lead to unpredictable behavior. Note


**Syntax:**

```c
long sysGetVariableFloatArray(char namespace[], char variable[], float values[], long arraySize); // form 1
```

```c
long sysGetVariableFloatArray(SysVarName, float values[], long arraySize); // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `namespace` | Name of the namespace. |
| `variable` | Name of the variable. |
| `values` | Gets the values of the variable. |
| `arraySize` | Size of the array. |
| `SysVarName` | Name of the fully qualified name of the system variable, including all namespaces, separated by "::". The name must be preceded by "sysVar::". |


**Return Value:** 0 no error, function successful 1 namespace was not found or second try to define the same namespace 2 variable was not found or second try to define the same variable 3 no writing right for the namespace available 4 the variable has no suitable type for the function


**Example(s):**

*Example 1*

```c
float fVarArr[10]; // array size should match the size of the system variable ... sysGetVariableFloatArray(sysvar::MyNamespace::FloatArrayVar, fVarArr, elcount(fVarArr));
```

*Example 2*

> This example shows the use of system variable types as a return value of functions and as a local variable. The user-defined CAPL function getSysVarFloatArray returns any system variable of type FloatArray . The function is therefore defined with the system variable type sysvarFloatArray as the return value. The system variable sv1 (defined with system variable type FloatArray ) represents any system variable depending on the counter of the getSysVarFloatArray function. The value of a system variable is set with the CAPL function sysSetVariableFloatArray . The value of the system variable is get with the CAPL function sysGetVariableFloatArray and output in the Write Window.

*Note*

```c
on key 'g' { sysvarFloatArray * sv1; float fVarArr [5] = {7.3, 7.3, 7.3, 7.3, 7.3}; sv1 = getSysVarFloatArray(0); write("Variable is %s%s, Value is %2.1lf", sv1.name, "[0]", @sv1[0]); sysSetVariableFloatArray(sv1,fVarArr, elcount(fVarArr)); write("Variable is %s%s, Value is: %2.1lf", sv1.name, "[0]",@sv1[0]); sv1 = getSysVarFloatArray(1); write("Variable is %s%s, Valgue is %2.1lf", sv1.name, "[0]", @sv1[0]); sv1 = getSysVarFloatArray(2); sysGetVariableFloatArray (sv1, fVarArr, elcount(fVarArr)); write("Variable is %s%s, Value is %2.1lf", sv1.name, "[0]",fVarArr[0]); } sysvarFloatArray * getSysVarFloatArray (int gKey) { switch (gKey) { case 0: return sysvar::FMW1::KeyFloatArray; case 1: return sysvar::DCM::SpeedSignalFloatArray; default: return sysvar::Engine::EngineFloatArray; } }
```

> You must define the system variables FMW1::KeyFloatArray , DCM::SpeedSignalFloatArray , and Engine::EngineFloatArray in the System Variables Configuration dialog first.


---

### sysGetVariableLong

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableLong.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableLong.htm)

**Description:**

Returns the value of a system variable of type long (32bit signed integer). Note The function can also be used for variables of type unsigned integer. You can simply cast the result to dword.


**Syntax:**

```c
long sysGetVariableLong (char namespace[], char variable[]); // form 1
```

```c
long sysGetVariableLong (SysVarName); // form 2
```

```c
long sysGetVariableLong (char namespace[], char variable[], long& value); // form 3
```

```c
long sysGetVariableLong (SysVarName, long& value); // form 4
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `namespace` | Name of the namespace. |
| `variable` | Name of the variable. |
| `SysVarName` | Name of the fully qualified name of the system variable, including all namespaces, separated by "::". The name must be preceded by "sysVar::". |
| `value` | Receives the current value of the variable. |


**Return Value:** Value of the variable or 0 in case of error (form 1 and 2) 0 No error, function successful (form 3 and 4) 2 Variable was not found (form 3 and 4) 4 Variable has no suitable type for the function (form 3 and 4)


**Example(s):**

*Example 1*

```c
sysGetVariableLong ("MyNamespace", "IntVar"); // example for form 1
sysGetVariableLong (sysvar::MyNamespace::IntVar); // example for form 2
```

*Example 2*

> This example shows the use of system variable types as a return value of functions and as a local variable. The user-defined CAPL function getSysVar returns any system variable of type Int . The function is therefore defined with the system variable type sysvarInt as the return value. The system variable sv1 (defined with system variable type Int ) represents any system variable depending on the counter of the getSysVar function. The value of a system variable is set with the CAPL function sysSetVariableLong . The value of the system variable is get with the CAPL function sysGetVariableLong and output in the Write Window.

*Note*

```c
on key 'a' { sysvarInt * sv1; //defines a local system variable 'sv1' with the system variable type 'sysvarInt' int  valueSysVar; sv1 = getSysVar(0); write("Variable is %s, Value is %d", sv1.name, @sv1); //@sv1++; sysSetVariableLong(sv1, @sv1+1); write("Variable is %s, Value is higher: %d", sv1.name, @sv1); sv1 = getSysVar(1); write("Variable is %s, Value is %d", sv1.name, @sv1); sv1 = getSysVar(2); valueSysVar = sysGetVariableLong(sv1); write("Variable is %s, Value is %d", sv1.name, valueSysVar); } sysvarInt * getSysVar(int aKey) { switch (aKey) { case 0: return sysvar::FMW1::Key; case 1: return sysvar::DCM::SpeedSignal; default: return sysvar::Engine::EngineStateSwitch; } }
```

> You must define the system variables FMW1::Key , DCM::SpeedSignal , and Engine::EngineStateSwitch in the System Variables Configuration dialog first.


---

### sysGetVariableLongArray

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableLongArray.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableLongArray.htm)

**Description:**

Returns the value of a variable of the long[] type. If the actual array size of the system variable is larger than the array size given as parameter, all elements in the values array up to the array size given as parameter will receive the current values of the system variable. Elements in the values array beyond the array size given as parameter will remain unchanged. If the actual array size of the system variable is smaller than the array size given as parameter, additional elements in the values array will be set to 0. Elements in the values array beyond the array size given as parameter will remain unchanged. Never give as parameter an array size which is larger than real size of the values array, this would lead to unpredictable behavior. Note


**Syntax:**

```c
long sysGetVariableLongArray(char namespace[], char variable[], long values[], long arraySize); // form 1
```

```c
long sysGetVariableLongArray(SysVarName, long values[], long arraySize); // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `namespace` | Name of the namespace. |
| `variable` | Name of the variable. |
| `values` | Values of the variable. |
| `arraySize` | Number of values in the array. |
| `SysVarName` | Name of the fully qualified name of the system variable, including all namespaces, separated by "::". The name must be preceded by "sysVar::". |


**Return Value:** 0 no error, function successful 1 namespace was not found or second try to define the same namespace 2 variable was not found or second try to define the same variable 3 no writing right for the namespace available 4 the variable has no suitable type for the function


**Example(s):**

*Example 1*

```c
long lVarArr[10]; // array size should match the size of the system variable ... sysGetVariableLongArray(sysvar::MyNamespace::LongArrayVar, lVarArr, elcount(lVarArr));
```

*Example 2*

> This example shows the use of system variable types as a return value of functions and as a local variable. The user-defined CAPL function getSysVarIntArray returns any system variable of type IntArray . The function is therefore defined with the system variable type sysvarIntArray as the return value. The system variable sv1 (defined with system variable type IntArray ) represents any system variable depending on the counter of the getSysVarIntArray function. The value of a system variable is set with the CAPL function sysSetVariableLongArray . The value of the system variable is get with the CAPL function sysGetVariableLongArray and output in the Write Window.

*Note*

```c
on key 'f' { sysvarIntArray * sv1; long lVarArr [5] = {7, 7, 7, 7, 7}; sv1 = getSysVarIntArray(0); write("Variable is %s%s, Value is %d", sv1.name, "[0]", @sv1[0]); sysSetVariableLongArray(sv1,lVarArr, elcount(lVarArr)); write("Variable is %s%s, Value is: %d", sv1.name, "[0]",@sv1[0]); sv1 = getSysVarIntArray(1); write("Variable is %s%s, Value is %d", sv1.name, "[0]", @sv1[0]); sv1 = getSysVarIntArray(2); sysGetVariableLongArray (sv1, lVarArr, elcount(lVarArr)); write("Variable is %s%s, Value is %d", sv1.name, "[0]",lVarArr[0]); } sysvarIntArray * getSysVarIntArray (int fKey) { switch (fKey) { case 0: return sysvar::FMW1::KeyIntegerArray; case 1: return sysvar::DCM::SpeedSignalIntegerArray; default: return sysvar::Engine::EngineIntegerArray; } }
```

> You must define the system variables FMW1::KeyIntegerArray , DCM::SpeedSignalIntegerArray , and Engine::EngineIntegerArray in the System Variables Configuration dialog first.


---

### sysGetVariableLongLong

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableLongLong.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableLongLong.htm)

**Description:**

Returns the value of a variable of a 64 bit integer type. Note The function can also be used for variables of type unsigned 64bit integer. You can simply cast the result to qword.


**Syntax:**

```c
int64 sysGetVariableLongLong(char namespace[], char variable[]); // form 1
```

```c
int64 sysGetVariableLongLong(SysVarName); // form 2
```

```c
long sysGetVariableLongLong(char namespace[], char variable[], int64& value); // form 3
```

```c
long sysGetVariableLongLong(SysVarName, int64& value); // form 4
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `namespace` | Name of the namespace. |
| `variable` | Name of the variable. |
| `value` | Receives the current value of the variable. |
| `SysVarName` | Name of the fully qualified name of the system variable, including all namespaces, separated by "::". The name must be preceded by "sysVar::". |


**Return Value:** Value of the variable or 0 in case of error (form 1 and 2) 0 no error, function successful (form 3 and 4) 2 variable was not found or second try to define the same variable (form 3 and 4) 4 the variable has no suitable type for the function (form 3 and 4)


**Example(s):**

```c
sysGetVariableLongLong(sysvar::MyNamespace::Int64Var);
```


---

### sysGetVariableMax

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableMax.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableMax.htm)

**Description:**

Retrieves the maximum of a variable. Note The function can also be used for specific elements of a system variable of type struct or generic array . For this, add the element to the name of the variable. If you directly give the element name to the function instead of using strings, precede the name by sysvarMember:: instead of sysvar:: . Example : sysvarMember::SomeNamespace::SomeStructVariable.SomeArrayMember[0]


**Syntax:**

```c
long sysGetVariableMax(SysVarName, long& maxValue); // form 1
```

```c
long sysGetVariableMax(SysVarName, double& maxValue); // form 2
```

```c
long sysGetVariableMax(char namespace[], char variable[], long& maxValue); // form 3
```

```c
long sysGetVariableMax(char namespace[], char variable[], double& maxValue); // form 4
```

```c
long sysGetVariableMax(SysVarName, dword& maxValue); // form 5
```

```c
long sysGetVariableMax(char namespace[], char variable[], dword& maxValue); // form 6
```

```c
long sysGetVariableMax(SysVarName, int64& maxValue); // form 7
```

```c
long sysGetVariableMax(char namespace[], char variable[], int64& maxValue); // form 8
```

```c
long sysGetVariableMax(SysVarName, qword& maxValue); // form 9
```

```c
long sysGetVariableMax(char namespace[], char variable[], qword& maxValue); // form 10
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `SysVarName` | Name of the fully qualified name of the system variable, including all namespaces, separated by "::". The name must be preceded by "sysVar::". |
| `namespace` | Name of the namespace. |
| `variable` | Name of the variable. |
| `maxValue` | Receives the maximum of the variable if no errors occur (reference parameter). Must be the correct type for the system variable. |


**Return Value:** 0 success 4 variable is of the wrong type or has no maximum defined


**Example(s):**

```c
—
```

**Description:**

Retrieves the minimum of a variable of type integer or integer array. Note The function can also be used for specific elements of a system variable of type struct or generic array . For this, add the element to the name of the variable. If you directly give the element name to the function instead of using strings, precede the name by sysvarMember:: instead of sysvar:: . Example : sysvarMember::SomeNamespace::SomeStructVariable.SomeArrayMember[0]




```c
long sysGetVariableMin(char namespace[], char variable[], double& minValue); // form 4
```

```c
long sysGetVariableMin(SysVarName, dword& minValue); // form 5
```

```c
long sysGetVariableMin(char namespace[], char variable[], dword& minValue); // form 6
```

```c
long sysGetVariableMin(SysVarName, int64& minValue); // form 7
```

```c
long sysGetVariableMin(char namespace[], char variable[], int64& minValue); // form 8
```

```c
long sysGetVariableMin(SysVarName, qword& minValue); // form 9
```

```c
long sysGetVariableMin(char namespace[], char variable[], qword& minValue); // form 10
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `SysVarName` | Name of the fully qualified name of the system variable, including all namespaces, separated by "::". The name must be preceded by "sysVar::". |
| `namespace` | Name of the namespace. |
| `variable` | Name of the variable. |
| `minValue` | Receives the minimum of the variable if no errors occur (reference parameter). Must be the correct type for the system variable. |


**Return Value:** 0 success 4 variable is of the wrong type or has no minimum defined

**Description:**

Returns the value of a variable of type unsigned 64 bit integer. Note

```c

```

```c
qword sysGetVariableQWord(SysVarName); // form 2
```


**Return Value:** Value of the variable or 0 in case of error (form 1 and 2) 0 no error, function successful (form 3 and 4) 2 variable was not found or second try to define the same variable (form 3 and 4) 4 the variable has no suitable type for the function (form 3 and 4)

### sysGetVariableString

> 🔗 [https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableString.htm](https://help.vector.com/CANoeDEFamily/19/en/Help/Content/Topics/CAPLFunctions/SystemVariables/Functions/CAPLfunctionSysGetVariableString.htm)

**Description:**

Returns the value of a variable of the String (char[]) type. Note


**Syntax:**

```c
long sysGetVariableString(char namespace[], char variable[], char buffer[], long bufferSize); // form 1
```

```c
long sysGetVariableString(SysVarName,char buffer[], long bufferSize); // form 2
```


**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `namespace` | Name of the namespace. |
| `variable` | Name of the variable. |
| `buffer` | Buffer that takes the value of the variable. |
| `bufferSize` | Size of the buffer. |
| `SysVarName` | Name of the fully qualified name of the system variable, including all namespaces, separated by "::". The name must be preceded by "sysVar::". |


**Return Value:** 0 no error, function successful 1 namespace was not found or second try to define the same namespace 2 variable was not found or second try to define the same variable 3 no writing right for the namespace available 4 the variable has no suitable type for the function


**Example(s):**

*Example 1*

```c
sysGetVariableString(sysvar::MyNamespace::StringVar, buf, elcount(buf));
```

*Example 2*

> This example shows the use of system variable types as a return value of functions and as a local variable. The user-defined CAPL function getSysVarString returns any system variable of type String . The function is therefore defined with the system variable type sysvarString as the return value. The system variable sv1 (defined with system variable type String ) represents any system variable depending on the counter of the getSysVarString function. The value of a system variable is set with the CAPL function sysSetVariableString . The value of the system variable is get with the CAPL function sysGetVariableString and output in the Write Window.

*Note*

```c
on key 'd' { sysvarString * svString1; char valueSysVarString[100]; char buf[100]; svString1 = getSysVarString(0); sysGetVariableString(svString1, buf, elcount(buf)); write("Variable is %s, Value is %s", svString1.name, buf); sysSetVariableString(svString1, "OFF"); sysGetVariableString(svString1, buf, elcount(buf)); write("Variable is %s, Value is now: %s", svString1.name, buf); svString1 = getSysVarString(1); sysGetVariableString(svString1, buf, elcount(buf)); write("Variable is %s, Value is %s", svString1.name, buf); svString1 = getSysVarString(2); sysGetVariableString(svString1, buf, elcount(buf)); write("Variable is %s, Value is %s", svString1.name, buf); } sysvarString * getSysVarString(int cKey) { switch (cKey) { case 0: return sysvar::FMW1::KeyString; case 1: return sysvar::DCM::SpeedSignalString; default: return sysvar::Engine::EngineStateString; } }
sysGetVariableString(sysvar::MyNamespace::StringVar, buf, elcount(buf));
```

> You must define the system variables FMW1::KeyString , DCM::SpeedSignalString , and Engine::EngineString in the System Variables Configuration dialog first.
